figures.name: Nancy Khedouri
figures.title: Author and Politician
figures.roleDescription: Bahraini Jewish author and parliamentarian
figures.significance: She is a prominent Jewish Bahraini author who wrote about the Jewish community's history and was appointed to parliament in 2010.
figures.biography:
Nancy Khedouri is a Jewish Bahraini author who wrote 'From Our Beginning to Present Day' about the Bahraini Jewish community, documenting their integration into Bahraini society. In her work, she explained how Jewish men were traditionally traders while women worked as teachers and nurses, developing strong bonds with local citizens. She has been an advocate for religious tolerance in Bahrain and has praised the peaceful coexistence between Jews and Bahrainis under King Hamad bin Isa Al-Khalifa's leadership.

In November 2010, Khedouri was appointed to replace Houda Nonoo in Parliament, continuing the tradition of Jewish representation in Bahraini political institutions. Her family business involves importing tablecloths and linens, making them one of Bahrain's leading importers in this sector. Through her writing and public statements, she has highlighted how Bahrain has practiced religious tolerance and provided peace and security to all its citizens, emphasizing the special relationship between the Jewish community and the broader Bahraini society.
achievements: Authored 'From Our Beginning to Present Day' about Bahraini Jewish community

figures.name: Virginie Kalantarian Mamoulian
figures.title: Theater Director
figures.roleDescription: Armenian theatre director and matriarch
figures.significance: Director of Armenian theatre and mother of acclaimed director Rouben Mamoulian, from a wealthy family of landowners and financiers.
figures.biography:
Virginie Kalantarian Mamoulian was from a family of wealthy landowners and financiers in the Armenian community of Tiflis, Russian Empire. She served as a director of the Armenian theatre, demonstrating the family's deep involvement in cultural and artistic pursuits. She married Zachary Mamoulian, a bank president, and together they raised their son Rouben and daughter Svetlana in the Armenian Apostolic faith.

The family was sympathetic to the Czarist regime and fled Russia during the turmoil of the 1917 revolution and ensuing civil war. Earlier, in the aftermath of the Russian Revolution of 1905, when ethnic violence arose in Tiflis, the family had moved to Paris for three years. Virginie's influence on her son's artistic development was significant, as the family maintained strong cultural connections and multilingual capabilities throughout their various relocations.

figures.name: Zachary Mamoulian
figures.title: Bank President
figures.roleDescription: Armenian banker and patriarch
figures.significance: Bank president and father of acclaimed director Rouben Mamoulian, who fled Russia during the 1917 revolution due to Czarist sympathies.
figures.biography:
Zachary Mamoulian was a prominent Armenian bank president in Tiflis, Russian Empire (now Tbilisi, Georgia). He married Virginie Kalantarian, who came from a wealthy family of landowners and financiers and served as a director of Armenian theatre. Together they raised their children Rouben and Svetlana in the Armenian Apostolic faith, creating a multilingual household where Russian, Armenian, and Georgian were spoken.

The family was forced to relocate multiple times due to political upheaval. After the Russian Revolution of 1905 and subsequent ethnic violence in Tiflis, they moved to Paris for three years. Later, the Mamoulian family, being sympathetic to the Czarist regime, fled Russia during the turmoil of the 1917 revolution and the ensuing civil war. Zachary's position and the family's wealth allowed them to maintain their cultural connections and provide excellent education for their children despite these disruptions.

figures.name: Svetlana Mamoulian
figures.title: Family Member
figures.roleDescription: Younger sister of director Rouben Mamoulian
figures.significance: Younger sister of acclaimed director Rouben Mamoulian, raised in an Armenian family that fled Russia during the 1917 revolution.
figures.biography:
Svetlana Mamoulian was the younger sister of director Rouben Mamoulian, born to Zachary and Virginie Kalantarian Mamoulian in Tiflis, Russian Empire. She was raised in the Armenian Apostolic faith alongside her brother in a multilingual household where Russian, Armenian, and Georgian were spoken fluently.

Like her brother, Svetlana experienced the family's multiple relocations due to political upheaval in Russia. The family moved to Paris for three years following the Russian Revolution of 1905 and ethnic violence in Tiflis, and later fled Russia entirely during the 1917 revolution due to their Czarist sympathies. Her upbringing in this culturally rich but politically turbulent environment shaped the family's later emphasis on artistic and cultural pursuits.

figures.name: Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi
figures.title: Mother of the Sudairi Seven
figures.roleDescription: Most influential wife of King Abdulaziz and matriarch of the Sudairi Seven
figures.significance: She was the most valued spouse of King Abdulaziz, bearing seven sons who became the most powerful group of full brothers in Saudi royal history.
figures.biography:
Hussa bint Ahmed was born in Al Ghat in 1900 to Ahmed bin Mohammed Al Sudairi, a powerful chief of the Sudairi tribe and early supporter of King Abdulaziz. She married King Abdulaziz twice - first in 1913 when she was thirteen, then again in 1920 after a brief marriage to King Abdulaziz's half-brother Muhammad bin Abdul Rahman. Her second marriage to King Abdulaziz produced seven sons known as the Sudairi Seven and four daughters.

Hussa was described as very beautiful with charm and a strong personality. She was influential in King Abdulaziz's decisions and urged him to make Prince Fahd a member of his advisory board. She organized daily dinner gatherings at her home for her sons and their families to maintain unity among them. Bandar bin Sultan described her as a combination of Margaret Thatcher and Mother Teresa. She died in Riyadh in 1969 at age 69, with funeral prayers attended by King Faisal and Crown Prince Khalid.
achievements: Mother of seven sons who became powerful Saudi princes
Organized family unity gatherings that strengthened the Sudairi faction
Most influential wife of King Abdulaziz
Raised Bandar bin Sultan, future Saudi ambassador to the US

figures.name: Abdul Rahman bin Abdulaziz Al Saud
figures.title: Deputy Minister of Defense and Aviation
figures.roleDescription: Long-serving deputy defense minister and member of the Sudairi Seven
figures.significance: The third-oldest of the Sudairi Seven who served as Deputy Minister of Defense under his brother Sultan for over three decades.
figures.biography:
Born in 1931, Abdul Rahman was appointed as Deputy Minister of Defense and Aviation under his full brother Prince Sultan, serving in this position from 1978 to 2011. His appointment was part of the Sudairi consolidation of power within key government portfolios, as the brothers placed family members in strategic positions within their respective ministries.

Abdul Rahman was reportedly disappointed when he was replaced by Prince Sultan's son Prince Khalid as deputy defense minister in 2011, as he had argued that he should have been promoted instead of his younger brother Salman when government reshuffles occurred. He was removed from the line of succession and died in 2017. Following his death, only the two youngest of the Sudairi Seven - Salman and Ahmed - remained alive, with Ahmed under house arrest by order of King Salman.
achievements: Deputy Minister of Defense and Aviation (1978-2011)
Served under brother Sultan for over three decades
Part of Sudairi consolidation of defense portfolio

figures.name: Ahmed bin Abdulaziz Al Saud
figures.title: Former Interior Minister
figures.roleDescription: Youngest of the Sudairi Seven and former deputy interior minister
figures.significance: The youngest of the Sudairi Seven who served as deputy interior minister for decades and is currently under house arrest.
figures.biography:
Born in 1942, Ahmed is the youngest of the Sudairi Seven and served as Deputy Minister of Interior under his brother Prince Nayef from 1975 to 2012. When Nayef became Crown Prince in 2011, Ahmed was reportedly concerned that Nayef was actively promoting the interests of his own son Mohammed rather than the broader Sudairi family interests.

When Nayef died in June 2012, Ahmed was briefly appointed Interior Minister on June 18, 2012, but resigned from the post on November 5, 2012, and was succeeded by Mohammed bin Nayef, Nayef's son. Ahmed became one of only three members of the ruling family to oppose Mohammad bin Salman becoming Crown Prince in 2017. As of the research period, Ahmed is under house arrest by order of his brother King Salman, making Salman the only politically active surviving member of the original Sudairi Seven.
achievements: Deputy Minister of Interior (1975-2012)
Interior Minister (June-November 2012)
Longest-serving deputy interior minister
Last surviving Sudairi Seven member besides King Salman

figures.name: Ahmed bin Mohammed Al Sudairi
figures.title: Tribal Chief and Provincial Governor
figures.roleDescription: Powerful Sudairi tribal chief and father of Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi
figures.significance: He was the patriarch of the Sudairi family whose daughter Hussa became the mother of the Sudairi Seven, making him the maternal grandfather of Saudi Arabia's most powerful princely faction.
figures.biography:
Ahmed bin Mohammed Al Sudairi (1869-1936) was a powerful chief of the Sudairi tribe and one of the early supporters of King Abdulaziz during his campaigns to conquer and unify Saudi Arabia. As a tribal leader, he played a crucial role in the political alliances that helped establish the modern Saudi state. Following the formation of the state, King Abdulaziz appointed him as governor of several provinces including Washm, Sudair, Qassim, and Aflaj.

His most lasting legacy came through his daughter Hussa bint Ahmed, who became King Abdulaziz's most influential wife and bore seven sons known as the Sudairi Seven. His other sons were also appointed by King Abdulaziz as governors of various provinces: Turki bin Ahmed governed Asir Province, Abdulaziz bin Ahmed governed Quraiyat al Milh and Wadi Sirhan, Khalid bin Ahmed governed Tabuk and later Najran Province and served as Minister of Agriculture, Muhammad bin Ahmed governed Northern Province, Abdul Rahman bin Ahmad governed Jauf, and Musa'id bin Ahmed governed Jizan Province.
achievements: Powerful chief of Sudairi tribe
Early supporter of King Abdulaziz
Governor of multiple provinces
Father of Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi

figures.biography:
Selim Zilkha was born in 1927 in Baghdad to an Iraqi Jewish family. He became a prominent entrepreneur who founded Mothercare, which grew to become one of the United Kingdom's largest retail chains specializing in products for mothers and children. His success with Mothercare established him as a significant figure in British retail.

Currently, Zilkha is the co-owner of Zilkha Biomass Energy, which owns timberland and manufactures pellet biofuel in Texas, showing his continued involvement in business ventures focused on sustainable energy. His career represents the successful transition of Iraqi Jewish business families to Western markets following the upheavals in the Middle East during the mid-20th century.

figures.name: Nathaniel Zilkha
figures.title: Businessman and Music Industry Executive
figures.roleDescription: Co-founder and executive chairman of Firebird Music Holdings and chairman of Gibson
figures.significance: Prominent music industry investor who led Gibson's recovery from bankruptcy and co-founded Firebird Music Holdings with access to $1bn in capital.
figures.biography:
Nathaniel 'Nat' Zilkha was born in 1975 in New York and raised in Portland, Maine. He graduated from Princeton University in 1999 with an AB in politics. A guitarist since grade school, in 1998 he co-founded the band Red Rooster with Princeton classmate Jay Erickson. The folk group evolved into an eight-piece jam band that released three albums and performed at venues including the Newport Folk Festival in 2008.

Zilkha began his finance career at Goldman Sachs, becoming vice president in 2006 before joining KKR in 2007. At KKR, he was appointed head of asset management and global special situations in 2010, became a partner and head of KKR Credit in 2013. In 2018, when KKR acquired majority stake in the bankrupt Gibson Brands, Zilkha served as chairman overseeing the company's return to solvency. As a guitarist, he felt 'bringing Gibson back to what it was supposed to be was almost a responsibility.'

In 2021, Zilkha left KKR to co-found Firebird Music Holdings with former Ticketmaster CEO Nathan Hubbard. With $1bn in capital access, Firebird has invested in Red Light Management, Defected Records, and created partnerships with the D'Amelio family. He also led a $50 million funding round for TVG Hospitality. Zilkha serves on boards including Save the Music Foundation and Apollo Theater.
achievements: Co-founder and executive chairman of Firebird Music Holdings
Chairman of Gibson Brands
Former KKR partner and head of KKR Credit
Led Gibson's recovery from bankruptcy

figures.name: Gabrielle Zilkha
figures.title: Filmmaker
figures.roleDescription: Toronto-based filmmaker and documentary maker
figures.significance: Documentary filmmaker who researched the House of Israel community in Ghana for a film project.
figures.biography:
Gabrielle Zilkha is a Toronto-based filmmaker who has worked on documentary projects exploring Jewish communities. In 2012, she visited Sefwe Wiawso in Ghana to conduct research for a documentary about the House of Israel community that she was producing. According to her research, about 200 people—mostly children—live in the House of Israel community in southwestern Ghana.

Zilkha noted that the lack of a historical record makes it difficult to verify the community's claims to Jewish ancestry, though there is an oral tradition dating back 200 years among the Sefwi tribe members who built a synagogue in 1998 and claim roots in the Ten Lost Tribes of ancient Israel.
achievements: Documentary filmmaker researching Jewish communities

figures.title: Businessman and Television Personality

figures.significance: Prominent Emirati businessman involved in major football club acquisitions and real estate ventures.
figures.biography:
Sulaiman Abdul-Karim Mohammed Al-Fahim was born in Dubai in 1976 or 1977 to a merchant family. He obtained his master's degree in Finance and Real Estate from the American University in Washington, D.C., though his claims of having a PhD from the same institution could not be verified. In 1998, tragedy struck when Al-Fahim lost his parents and brother in a car accident.

Al-Fahim became head of the real estate company Hydra Properties, which is owned by the Royal Group, an investment conglomerate headed by Tahnoun bin Zayed Al Nahyan. He gained international prominence for his role in facilitating the Abu Dhabi royal family's acquisition of Manchester City F.C. in 2008, where he signed the initial Memorandum of Understanding. He also briefly owned Portsmouth F.C. for six weeks in 2009. Beyond football, Al-Fahim served as president of UAE Chess Federation from 2008 to 2012 and founded the business reality television show 'Hydra Executives' which ran for two seasons.

However, his career was marred by legal troubles when in 2018, he was convicted and sentenced to five years in jail by a UAE court for stealing £5 million from his wife to fund the Portsmouth purchase. This conviction significantly damaged his reputation and highlighted the risks associated with his business practices.
achievements: Facilitated Abu Dhabi royal family's acquisition of Manchester City F.C.

Badr Al Ghurair is the Chief Executive Officer of Cars Taxi, which operates as the largest taxi fleet provider in Dubai and is part of Al Ghurair's mobility sector. In his role, Badr oversees the ongoing operations of all divisions within the company. Under his leadership, Cars Taxi became notable as the first taxi company in the UAE to incorporate hybrid taxis into their fleet, demonstrating innovation in sustainable transportation.

Badr is the son of Abdulla bin Ahmad Al Ghurair, the founder and chairman of Mashreq Bank, positioning him as part of the third generation of the Al Ghurair business family. His role in the mobility sector represents the family's diversification beyond their traditional areas of banking, construction, and manufacturing into modern transportation services.

figures.significance: Made significant donation to renovate the Paul Findley Congressional Office Museum at Illinois College in 2007.
figures.biography:
Mohammed Al Habtoor is a notable philanthropist who has made significant contributions to educational institutions. In 2007, he made a substantial donation that enabled the renovation of the Paul Findley Congressional Office Museum at Illinois College in Jacksonville, Illinois. The museum, housed in Whipple Hall on the college campus, is dedicated to former congressman and alumnus Paul Findley and contains artifacts related to Findley's political career, his interest in Abraham Lincoln, and his involvement in human rights and Middle East issues.

Al Habtoor's donation was instrumental in preserving and enhancing this important historical collection, which includes Lincoln's 1837 law office sofa, Findley's congressional desk, WWI and campaign memorabilia, and gifts from seven U.S. presidents and international leaders. The museum serves as an educational resource that is open for tours and visits by appointment, continuing to benefit students and visitors through Al Habtoor's generosity.
achievements: Donated funds for renovation of Paul Findley Congressional Office Museum at Illinois College in 2007

figures.name: Rashid Al-Habtoor
figures.title: UAE Businessman
figures.roleDescription: Business executive and hotel industry investor
figures.significance: Submitted a major bid for the acquisition of Leela Hotels & Resorts in India in 2019.
figures.biography:
Rashid Al-Habtoor is a prominent UAE businessman who has been involved in significant international business transactions, particularly in the hospitality sector. In January 2019, he emerged as one of the major suitors for the acquisition of Leela Hotels & Resorts, a prestigious Indian luxury hotel chain. Al-Habtoor, working jointly with business partner Abhishek Verma, submitted a substantial bid of US$600 million for the hotel group.

The bid was part of a competitive acquisition process that included other major suitors such as ITC Group and ultimately Brookfield Asset Management, which eventually won the acquisition. Despite the significant value of Al-Habtoor's offer, questions were raised about the conduct of JM Financial, the asset reconstruction company managing the sale transaction, regarding how various bids were entertained and evaluated. This high-profile attempt to acquire the Indian hotel chain demonstrated Al-Habtoor's active involvement in international hospitality investments and his capacity for major business transactions.
achievements: Submitted $600 million bid for Leela Hotels & Resorts acquisition in 2019

figures.significance: Leading Emirati businesswoman who ranked among the world's most powerful Arab women and serves in senior roles across the family business empire.
figures.biography:
Dr. Amina Al Rustamani is a prominent Emirati businesswoman who currently serves as Director and Board Member of AW Rostamani Group and Chief Executive Officer of AWR Properties. She earned her bachelor's, master's and doctoral degrees in electrical engineering from The George Washington University in 1993, 1996, and 2001, respectively. She started her career in Dubai in 2001 as an electrical engineer with TECOM Investments (now TECOM Group), where she was involved in the development of Dubai Design District and Dubai Wholesale City.

In February 2018, Al Rustamani left TECOM Group to take up the position as Chief Operating Officer of the AW Rostamani Group while also serving as CEO of AWR Properties, the real estate arm of the family business. She has achieved significant recognition for her business leadership, ranking number 9 in CEO Middle East's fifth annual list of the world's most powerful Arab women in 2015 and being picked as the Advertising Person of the Year by Dubai Lynx for 2015. She was also listed as number 20 in Forbes 'Middle East Power Businesswomen 2021.'

Beyond the family business, she serves on multiple high-profile boards including Dubai Healthcare City Authority, Noor Bank, and HSBC Bank Middle East. Her previous board positions included Dubai Media Incorporated, National Media Council, and Emirates Central Cooling Systems Corporation (Empower), and she served as Chairperson of the Dubai Design and Fashion Council.

figures.name: Abdullah Saleh Al Mulla
figures.title: Secretary of State and Business Founder
figures.roleDescription: Founder and patriarch of Al Mulla Group
figures.significance: Founded Al Mulla Group in 1938, establishing one of Kuwait's most prominent business dynasties.
figures.biography:
Abdullah Saleh Al Mulla served as Secretary of State and established Al Mulla Group in 1938 by setting up an electrical and domestic appliance store in Kuwait. He soon acquired the GEC franchise from the United Kingdom, marking the beginning of what would become one of Kuwait's largest diversified business groups. Under his leadership, the company expanded into various sectors including automotive, with the creation of Bader Al Mulla and Brothers Company in 1947, which acquired distribution rights for Chrysler Middle East, Plymouth and Dodge Middle East vehicles in Kuwait.

Al Mulla continued to expand the business into marine products, HVAC contracting, travel and industrial equipment distribution. His entrepreneurial vision laid the foundation for what would eventually become a business empire with operations in 8 countries and over 15,000 employees. Following his death in 1955, his business legacy was continued by his sons, with his eldest son Bader taking over as chairman.

figures.name: Bader Al Mulla
figures.title: Chairman of Al Mulla Group
figures.roleDescription: Eldest son and successor to founder Abdullah Saleh Al Mulla
figures.significance: Led Al Mulla Group as chairman from 1955-1969, continuing his father's business legacy.
figures.biography:
Bader Al Mulla was the eldest son of Abdullah Saleh Al Mulla, founder of Al Mulla Group. Following his father's death in 1955, Bader assumed leadership of the family business as chairman, a position he held until 1969. During his tenure, he oversaw the continued expansion of the Al Mulla Group's operations across various sectors including automotive, marine products, HVAC contracting, and industrial equipment distribution.

After stepping down as chairman in 1969, Bader was succeeded by his brothers Najeeb and Anwar, who took on the roles of Chairman and Deputy Chairman & CEO respectively. The company bearing his name, Bader Al Mulla and Brothers Company, had been established in 1947 and was instrumental in securing major automotive distribution rights in Kuwait. His leadership helped consolidate the business foundation laid by his father during the crucial post-independence period of Kuwait's economic development.
achievements: Served as Chairman of Al Mulla Group from 1955-1969
Continued expansion of family business operations
Maintained major automotive distribution partnerships

figures.name: Najeeb Al Mulla
figures.title: Chairman of Al Mulla Group
figures.roleDescription: Second-generation leader who became Chairman in 1969
figures.significance: Led Al Mulla Group during a period of significant expansion and modernization in the 1970s.
figures.biography:
Najeeb Al Mulla was one of the sons of founder Abdullah Saleh Al Mulla and brother to Bader and Anwar Al Mulla. In 1969, he became Chairman of Al Mulla Group, taking over from his elder brother Bader, while his brother Anwar assumed the role of Deputy Chairman & CEO. This marked a new phase of shared leadership within the family business.

Under Najeeb's chairmanship, the company underwent significant transformation and expansion. In 1972, three years after he took over, the Maseelah Trading Company was founded, which acquired the distribution rights for Mitsubishi Motor Vehicles in Kuwait, leading to dramatic growth in the group's automotive business. In 1978, Najeeb oversaw radical changes to the company structure, including the appointment of a multinational board instead of a board consisting only of family members. During this period, the Group entered additional businesses such as environmental systems, vehicle rental and leasing, fire protection, office equipment, security services, engineering products, consumer appliances and insurance consultancy.
achievements: Served as Chairman of Al Mulla Group from 1969
Established Maseelah Trading Company in 1972
Secured Mitsubishi Motor Vehicles distribution rights

figures.name: Anwar Al Mulla
figures.title: Deputy Chairman & CEO of Al Mulla Group
figures.roleDescription: Second-generation leader who served as Deputy Chairman & CEO from 1969
figures.significance: Played a key role in modernizing Al Mulla Group's operations and expanding into new business sectors.
figures.biography:
Anwar Al Mulla was one of the sons of founder Abdullah Saleh Al Mulla and brother to Bader and Najeeb Al Mulla. In 1969, when leadership of the family business transitioned from his elder brother Bader, Anwar took on the role of Deputy Chairman & CEO while his brother Najeeb became Chairman. This dual leadership structure marked a new era for the Al Mulla Group.

As Deputy Chairman & CEO, Anwar was instrumental in the operational management and strategic expansion of the company. He worked alongside his brother Najeeb during a period of significant growth and modernization. Under their joint leadership, the company established Maseelah Trading Company in 1972 and secured important distribution rights for Mitsubishi Motor Vehicles in Kuwait. Anwar also played a key role in the 1978 restructuring that introduced a multinational board and expanded the company into numerous new sectors including environmental systems, vehicle rental and leasing, fire protection, office equipment, security services, engineering products, consumer appliances and insurance consultancy.

figures.name: Isa Bin Abdullatif Al Serkal
figures.title: British Residency Agent
figures.roleDescription: British Native Residency Agent in Sharjah during the early-mid 20th century
figures.significance: Served as the key British representative and intermediary in Sharjah, playing a crucial role in regional politics and negotiations including the establishment of Sharjah Airport.
figures.biography:
Isa bin Abdullatif Al Serkal served as the British Residency Agent in Sharjah during a critical period of regional development in the early-to-mid 20th century. He acted as the primary intermediary between the British government and local rulers, representing British interests while managing complex tribal and political relationships. His role was particularly significant during the turbulent period involving Abdulrahman bin Muhammad Al Shamsi, the headman of Al Heera, when Al Serkal's cousin, Ibrahim bin Rajab, was killed by a shot fired in Sharjah on 11 October 1925. The attack was believed to have been aimed at Al Serkal himself, highlighting the dangerous nature of his position.

Al Serkal played a crucial role in the negotiations for the establishment of Sharjah Airport in the 1930s. He was a co-signatory to the historic agreement made on 22 June 1932 between the British government and Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi, the Ruler of Sharjah, which established the first airport in the Trucial States. The agreement stipulated that Imperial Airways would only deal with the Sheikh through the Residency Agent, emphasizing Al Serkal's central role in this groundbreaking aviation development. His position required him to balance British imperial interests with local sensitivities, often in challenging circumstances involving tribal conflicts, debt disputes, and territorial disagreements among the various rulers of the Trucial Coast.
achievements: Co-signed the historic 1932 agreement establishing Sharjah Airport, the first airport in the Trucial States
Served as key British intermediary in complex tribal and political negotiations

figures.name: Ali Hassan al-Majid
figures.title: Colonel General
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi military officer and politician, Defense Minister and Interior Minister
figures.significance: Notorious Iraqi military officer known as 'Chemical Ali' for his use of chemical weapons against Kurdish populations during the Al-Anfal campaign.
figures.biography:
Ali Hassan al-Majid al-Tikriti was born around 1941 in al-Awja near Tikrit, a first cousin of Saddam Hussein from the Al-Bu Nasir tribe. He worked as a motorcycle messenger and driver in the Iraqi Army from 1959 until the Ba'ath Party seized power in 1968, after which he gained entry into the Military Academy and was commissioned as an Infantry officer. His rise was aided by his cousin Saddam, initially becoming an aide to Defense Minister Hammadi Shihab in the early 1970s, then head of the government's Security Office.

During the late stages of the Iran-Iraq War, al-Majid served as Secretary General of the Northern Bureau of the Ba'ath Party from March 1987 to April 1989, effectively making him Saddam's proconsul in northern Iraq. In this role, he ordered the indiscriminate use of chemical weapons against Kurdish targets during the genocidal Al-Anfal campaign, earning him the nickname 'Chemical Ali.' The campaign resulted in the destruction of 4,000 villages and the killing of an estimated 180,000 Kurds. He later served as military governor of occupied Kuwait during the Gulf War and held various ministerial posts including Defense Minister and Interior Minister.

Following the 2003 invasion of Iraq, al-Majid was captured by American forces on 17 August 2003 in Basra. He was tried by the Iraqi Special Tribunal and received four death sentences for genocide, crimes against humanity, and war crimes. He was executed by hanging on 25 January 2010, having been convicted for his role in the Kurdish genocide, killing Shia Muslims, and the Halabja chemical attack.
achievements: Secretary General of the Northern Bureau of the Ba'ath Party (1987-1989)
Military Governor of Kuwait during Gulf War (1990)
Defense Minister of Iraq
Interior Minister of Iraq

figures.name: Saddam Kamel Hassan al-Majid
figures.title: Head of Republican Guard
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi military officer, first cousin once removed and son-in-law of Saddam Hussein
figures.significance: Head of Iraq's Republican Guard who defected with his brother in 1995 but was killed upon their return in 1996.
figures.biography:
Saddam Kamel Hassan al-Majid was born on 1 June 1960, the first cousin once removed and son-in-law of Iraqi president Saddam Hussein. He was married to Rana Hussein, Saddam's daughter, and was the brother of Hussein Kamel al-Majid, who was married to another of Saddam's daughters, Raghad Hussein. For a time, he served as head of the Republican Guard until he was removed from the position in 1986 in favor of Saddam Hussein's son, Qusay Hussein.

Due to his close resemblance to the Iraqi leader, Saddam Kamel played the part of Saddam Hussein in the propagandistic film 'The Long Days,' which depicted Saddam Hussein's early life and rise to power. In 1995, he defected from Iraq along with his brother Hussein Kamel and their wives, fleeing to Jordan. His brother provided information to UNSCOM, the CIA, and MI6 about Iraq's weapons of mass destruction programs during their exile.

In 1996, believing Saddam's assurances that they had been pardoned for their defection, the brothers returned to Iraq. However, shortly after their return on 23 February 1996, both Saddam Kamel and his brother Hussein Kamel were killed in a prolonged gun battle. According to Iraqi authorities, they were killed by other members of their clan who exercised personal vengeance upon the brothers, possibly as a way for Saddam to circumvent his promise of pardon by masking the executions as a family quarrel.
achievements: Head of Republican Guard
Actor in propaganda film 'The Long Days'
Part-time actor

figures.name: Hussein Kamel al-Majid
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi official, son-in-law of Saddam Hussein
figures.significance: Brother of Saddam Kamel who defected in 1995 and provided intelligence about Iraq's WMD programs to Western agencies before being killed in 1996.
figures.biography:
Hussein Kamel al-Majid was the brother of Saddam Kamel al-Majid and was married to Raghad Hussein, one of Saddam Hussein's daughters. He held a significant position within the Iraqi government and had access to sensitive information about the country's weapons programs. In 1995, he defected from Iraq along with his brother Saddam Kamel and their wives, seeking refuge in Jordan.

During his time in exile, Hussein Kamel provided valuable information to UNSCOM (United Nations Special Commission), the CIA, and MI6 about Iraq's weapons of mass destruction programs. His testimony and intelligence were considered crucial for understanding the extent and nature of Iraq's biological, chemical, and nuclear weapons development efforts during the 1980s and early 1990s.

In 1996, Hussein Kamel and his brother returned to Iraq after receiving assurances from Saddam Hussein that they would be pardoned for their defection. However, on 23 February 1996, shortly after their return, both brothers were killed in what Iraqi authorities described as a gun battle with clan members who considered them traitors. The killings were officially presented as family vengeance, though many believed it was orchestrated by Saddam as a way to eliminate the defectors while maintaining plausible deniability.

figures.name: Saddam Hussein Al-Majid Al-Tikriti
figures.title: President of Iraq
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi politician and revolutionary, President of Iraq from 1979 to 2003
figures.significance: Dominant Iraqi political leader for 35 years who ruled as President from 1979 until his overthrow in 2003, known for domestic modernization efforts and regional conflicts.
figures.biography:
Saddam Hussein Al-Majid Al-Tikriti was born on 28 April 1937 in al-Awja, a small village near Tikrit, to a Sunni Arab family from the Al-Bejat clan of the Al-Bu Nasir tribe. His father, Hussein Abd al-Majid, was from the Al-Majid branch of the Al-Bejat clan. After a difficult childhood, he was raised by his uncle Khairallah Talfah and joined the revolutionary Ba'ath Party in 1957 at age 20. He participated in a failed assassination attempt against Iraqi leader Abdul Karim Qasim in 1959, after which he fled to Egypt where he completed his education.

Saddam rose to power through the Ba'ath Party, becoming vice president in 1968 under Ahmed Hassan al-Bakr and formally assuming the presidency in 1979. During his rule, he implemented significant domestic reforms including nationalization of oil resources, infrastructure development, healthcare and education expansion, and women's rights advancement. Iraq experienced substantial economic growth in the 1970s, with the Iraqi dinar worth more than 3 US dollars at its peak. He also invested heavily in modernizing the country's infrastructure, bringing electricity to nearly every city and establishing one of the Middle East's most advanced healthcare systems.

However, Saddam's presidency was marked by three major military conflicts: the Iran-Iraq War (1980-1988), the invasion of Kuwait leading to the Gulf War (1991), and ongoing conflicts with Kurdish populations including the genocidal Al-Anfal campaign. His regime was characterized by authoritarian rule, with extensive use of surveillance, torture, and repression against political opponents. Following the 2003 US-led invasion of Iraq, he was captured, tried for crimes against humanity, and executed by hanging on 30 December 2006.
achievements: President of Iraq (1979-2003)
Vice President of Iraq (1968-1979)
Nationalized Iraq's oil industry (1972)
Established universal free education system
Developed modern healthcare infrastructure
Promoted women's rights and education
Received UNESCO award for healthcare system development

figures.biography:
Salem bin Ali al Owais (1887-1959) was one of the most prominent early Emirati writers and poets. He lived during a transformative period in UAE history, spanning the late pearling era through the early oil discoveries. Al Owais was part of a literary tradition that helped establish Emirati cultural identity through written works.

Along with Mubarak Al Oqaili (1880-1954) and Ahmed bin Sulayem (1905-1976), al Owais formed part of the foundational generation of Emirati literature. His work contributed to the early development of written Emirati culture during a time when the region was transitioning from a pearling-based economy to one that would eventually be transformed by oil wealth. His literary contributions helped preserve and develop Emirati cultural heritage during this crucial period of change.

figures.biography: Mukhtar Ahmed Ansari (1880-1936) was a prominent surgeon and political leader during India's independence movement. He served as president of the Muslim League in both 1918 and 1920, demonstrating his early leadership in Muslim political representation. Later, he became president of the Indian National Congress in 1927, showing his broader nationalist credentials and ability to bridge communal divides during a critical period in Indian politics. His dual leadership roles in both major political organizations of the time made him a significant figure in the struggle for Indian independence. As a medical professional, he combined his surgical career with political activism, representing the educated Muslim elite's participation in the nationalist movement.

figures.name: Mohammad Hamid Ansari
figures.title: Vice President of India
figures.roleDescription: Diplomat and Vice President of India (2007-2017)
figures.significance: Served as Vice President of India for two terms (2007-2017) with the longest vice presidential tenure in Indian history.
figures.biography: Mohammad Hamid Ansari was born on April 1, 1937, in Kolkata to Mohammad Abdul Aziz Ansari and Aasiya Begum. Though his ancestral home is in Yusufpur, Ghazipur, Uttar Pradesh, he spent his formative years in Kolkata. He comes from a family of Ansari sheikhs descended from the Sufi saint Abdullah Ansari of Herat, and is the grand-nephew of former Indian National Congress president Mukhtar Ahmed Ansari. Joining the Indian Foreign Service in 1961, Ansari served as ambassador to several countries including the UAE (1976-1980), Australia (1985-1989), Afghanistan (1989-1990), Iran (1990-1992), and Saudi Arabia (1995-1999). He also served as India's permanent representative to the United Nations from 1993 to 1995. After his diplomatic career, he became vice-chancellor of Aligarh Muslim University (2000-2002) and chairman of the National Commission for Minorities (2006-2007). Elected Vice President of India on August 10, 2007, he was re-elected in 2012, serving two full terms until 2017. His tenure is tied with Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan for the longest vice presidential tenure in Indian history.

figures.name: Mukhtar Ansari
figures.title: Politician and Gangster

figures.significance: Five-time MLA from Mau constituency who combined criminal activities with electoral politics in Uttar Pradesh.
figures.biography: Mukhtar Ansari (June 30, 1963 – March 28, 2024) was an Indian gangster, convicted murderer, and politician based in Uttar Pradesh. His paternal grandfather was Mukhtar Ahmed Ansari, the surgeon and early president of the Indian National Congress and Muslim League. His father was Subhan Ullah Ansari, a Communist party leader. The family claims patrilineal lineage from the 11th century Sufi saint Abdullah Ansari of Herat in Afghanistan. Ansari entered politics around 1995 through a student union and became an MLA in 1996, eventually being elected five times from the Mau constituency. He joined the Bahujan Samaj Party in 2007 along with his brother Afzal, but was expelled in 2010 after continued involvement in criminal activities. He then formed his own party, Quami Ekta Dal, in 2010 before rejoining the BSP in 2016. Throughout his political career, he faced numerous criminal cases and spent considerable time in jail from 2005 onwards. He died of a heart attack on March 28, 2024, while in prison at age 60, with his family alleging he was poisoned.

figures.name: Aziz Ansari
figures.title: Actor and Comedian
figures.roleDescription: American actor, comedian, and filmmaker
figures.significance: First Asian American actor to win a Golden Globe for acting on television for his role in Master of None.
figures.biography: Aziz Ismail Ansari was born on February 23, 1983, in Columbia, South Carolina, to Indian immigrants from Tamil Nadu. His parents are Tamil Muslims, though Ansari himself is non-religious. He graduated from NYU Stern School of Business in 2004 with a Bachelor of Business degree in marketing. Ansari began performing comedy in New York City while a student, frequently performing at the Upright Citizens Brigade Theatre. He co-created and starred in the MTV sketch comedy show Human Giant before landing his breakthrough role as Tom Haverford on NBC's Parks and Recreation (2009-2015). In 2015, he co-created and starred in Netflix's critically acclaimed series Master of None, for which he won several awards including two Emmys and a Golden Globe - making him the first Asian American actor to win a Golden Globe for acting on television. As a stand-up comedian, he has released multiple comedy specials and continues to tour. His first book, Modern Romance: An Investigation, was released in 2015, and he was included in Time's 100 most influential people list in 2016.

figures.roleDescription: U.S. Representative for Arizona's 3rd congressional district
figures.significance: First Iranian American elected to public office in Arizona and youngest female member of Congress.
figures.biography: Yassamin Ansari was born on April 7, 1992, in Seattle, Washington, to parents who immigrated to the United States after fleeing Iran following the Islamic Revolution. She grew up in Scottsdale, Arizona, and attended Chaparral High School, where she organized with the Arizona Democratic Party for Barack Obama's 2008 campaign. She attended Stanford University, receiving a bachelor's degree in international relations, and later earned a master's degree from St. John's College, Cambridge as a Gates Cambridge Scholar. After graduation, she worked in the UN Secretary-General's office under Ban Ki-moon and António Guterres, focusing on climate policy including the Paris Agreement. She was elected to the Phoenix City Council in 2021, becoming the youngest person elected to the council and the first Iranian American elected to public office in Arizona. During her tenure, she created an Office of Heat Response and Mitigation and promoted climate action initiatives. In 2024, she was elected to the U.S. House of Representatives for Arizona's 3rd congressional district, becoming the youngest female member of Congress.

figures.biography:
Salem Al Ketbi is a prominent Emirati political analyst, researcher and opinion writer from a Bedouin background. He holds a Ph.D. in Public Law and Political Science from Hassan II University, Casablanca, for his thesis on political and religious propaganda and leadership through social media in the Arab World.

Al Ketbi has written extensively for major newspapers and media outlets including Gulf News, The Jerusalem Post, Al Arabiya, and many others in both English and Arabic. He focuses on UAE national security, Iranian foreign policy, terrorist organizations and extremist groups. In 2017, he published a political biography of Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan titled 'Fakhr al-Oroba' which won the Arab Women Media Network award for Book of the Year.

He is a member of several prestigious organizations including Chatham House, the International Federation of Journalists, and various press clubs. In 2019, he ran for the Federal National Council election on a platform of education, women and youth empowerment. Al Ketbi has spoken at international forums and is recognized as an expert on Middle Eastern politics.

figures.biography:
Maitha Salem Al Shamsi is a politician and government minister in the United Arab Emirates who serves as Minister of State. She completed her Bachelor of Arts in 1980 from King Saud University in Saudi Arabia and her master's degree in 1988 from Alexandria University in Egypt. She also completed a post doctorate in Sociology and has written several books including 'Expatriate Migration to the Gulf Cooperation Council States: Current Problems and Future Prospects', 'the Mother of the Nation: Fatima bint Mubarak', and 'Principles and Accomplishments'.

In 2008 she joined the Federal Government of the United Arab Emirates as the Minister of State and helped establish the Sorbonne University in Abu Dhabi. She has held numerous leadership positions including serving on the board of trustees of Abu Dhabi University, sitting on UNESCO's Scientific Committee for the Arab States on Education and Scientific Research, and serving as chairperson of the Marriage Fund until February 2016. She currently serves as chairperson of the Family Development Foundation, the General Women's Union in United Arab Emirates, and the Supreme Council for Motherhood and Childhood. In 2017, she pledged seven million dollars to support Rohingya refugees at a United Nations conference in Geneva.

figures.name: Abdulrahman bin Muhammad Al Shamsi
figures.title: Sheikh of Al Heera
figures.roleDescription: Influential and controversial tribal leader in early 20th century Trucial States
figures.significance: He was a highly influential and divisive figure in regional politics during the early 20th century, described by British officials as 'a stormy petrel of the Trucial Coast, a man feared by everyone in and around Sharjah'.
figures.biography:
Abdulrahman bin Muhammad Al Shamsi was the Sheikh, or head man, of the township of Al Heera, today a suburb of Sharjah in the United Arab Emirates. A highly influential and often divisive figure in regional politics during the early 20th century in the Trucial States, he was frequently at odds with other rulers and often in trouble with the British administration. By 1920, facing enormous debts of Rs 21,560 from his town's struggling pearling fleet, he led a desperate attack on Ajman on 15 June 1920, occupying its fort for six days before being forced out by British intervention.

His conflicts led to multiple exiles, including a period with the Shihuh of the Rus Al Jibal mountains and later a four-year exile to Aden (returning after three years) following accusations of involvement in the 1925 killing of Ibrahim bin Rajab in Sharjah. Despite his turbulent relationships, Al Shamsi played a crucial role in the establishment of Sharjah Airport, with his influential backing among Bedouin tribes being instrumental in Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi's successful negotiations with the British for the airport in 1932. He ruled Al Heera until his death on 10 August 1942, when the township reverted to rule by Sharjah.

His business establishment gained international attention when it served as a clue location during the seventh leg of The Amazing Race 23 in 2013, where teams competing in the reality show had to find their next clue at his company.

The inclusion of his business in this internationally televised program highlights the Al Khoory family's established presence in the UAE's commercial sector. His company's location in the Irani Souk suggests connections to the broader Iranian merchant community that has historically played a significant role in the UAE's trade and commerce development.

figures.significance: Served as Deputy Prime Minister and twice as Minister of Finance under Saddam Hussein's Ba'athist government.
figures.biography:
Hikmat Mizban Ibrahim al-Azzawi was born in 1933 and originally trained as an economist. He was arrested in 1960 for protesting against the government of Iraqi General Abd al-Karim Qasim. He joined the Arab Socialist Ba'ath Party in 1968 and was named undersecretary of state for Commerce. His career faced significant turbulence when he was fired from all his official positions in 1982 and demoted to a low administrative job, being expelled from the party until 1985 when he became Governor of the Central Bank of Iraq. In April 1987, he was publicly humiliated and severely dismissed for refusing to transfer a large sum of money at the demand of one of Saddam's uncles.

President Saddam Hussein forced him back into the position of Minister of Finance in 1995. In July 1999, he was given the honorary title of Deputy Prime Minister. After the United States-led Invasion of Iraq in 2003, he was listed as number 45 (8 of Diamonds) on the Iraqi most-wanted playing cards. He was peacefully detained from his house in Baghdad on April 19, 2003 by Iraqi police and handed over to the US military. He died in prison on January 27, 2012, at age 79, due to lack of his heart medications, with his health deteriorating because of aging and cancer.
achievements: Served as Deputy Prime Minister of Iraq
Served twice as Minister of Finance under Saddam Hussein
Served as Governor of the Central Bank of Iraq

figures.name: Dia Azzawi
figures.title: Artist and Architect
figures.roleDescription: Contemporary Iraqi artist known for incorporating Arabic calligraphy and ancient Mesopotamian art
figures.significance: A prominent contemporary artist whose work includes references to Arabic calligraphy, Sumerian and Assyrian art, and has inspired many contemporary calligraffiti artists.
figures.biography:
Dia Azzawi was born in 1939 and became one of Iraq's most notable contemporary artists. His work is described as contemporary art that includes references to Arabic calligraphy, as well as Sumerian and Assyrian art. Azzawi has been a significant inspiration for many contemporary calligraffiti artists, helping to bridge traditional Iraqi artistic heritage with modern artistic expression.

Azzawi was one of the founding members of Al-Ru'yah al-Jadida (The New Vision) art group, established in 1968 alongside artists Ismail Fatah Al Turk, Muhammed Muhr al-Din, Hashimi al-Samarchi, Salih Jumai'e, and Rafa al-Nasiri. This group promoted the idea of freedom of creativity within a framework of heritage and represented a free art style where artists believed they needed to be true to their own era. He was also a founding member of the One Dimension Group, established around 1971, which sought a new artistic identity drawn from within Iraqi culture and heritage, blending Arabic motifs, especially calligraphy, and geometric shapes into contemporary and abstract artworks. Additionally, he collaborated with Mahmoud Obaidi, an Iraqi-Canadian artist, in building the structure that houses the Nabu Museum in Lebanon.
achievements: Co-founder of Al-Ru'yah al-Jadida (The New Vision) art group in 1968
Founding member of the One Dimension Group around 1971
Collaborated in building the Nabu Museum structure in Lebanon
Inspiration for contemporary calligraffiti artists

figures.name: Muhammad Sa'id al-Suwaidi
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Islamic scholar, poet, and mosque Imam
figures.significance: A prominent Iraqi Islamic scholar and leader of the Sufi Naqshbandi Order in Baghdad during the Ottoman period.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Muhammad Sa'id al-Suwaidi was born in Karkh, Baghdad, in 1728 to the Iraqi al-Suwaidi family, which was famous for its knowledge, jurisprudence, and literature. His father, Sheikh Abdullah bin Hussein al-Suwaidi, was the author of many books, including 'Al-Nafha al-Miskiyya' on the Meccan journey. He studied under his father as well as other scholars such as Sheikh Abd al-Qadir al-Makki al-Harithi and Sheikh Ali al-Ansari, and would temporarily visit Cairo's madrasas.

As a member and leader of the Sufi Naqshbandi Order in Baghdad, Ottoman Iraq, he became a significant religious figure. His house in Karkh was a notable landmark in Baghdad and was later inherited by his son, Muhammad Amin, who used it as a madrasa where he would teach. The upper floor overlooking the road served as a majlis. When the structure weakened, it was renovated and became known as al-Suwaidi Madrasa, remaining one of the famous schools in Karkh until Muhammad Amin's death in 1830. He died in 1808 and was buried in the Sheikh Ma'ruf Cemetery.
achievements: Leader of the Sufi Naqshbandi Order in Baghdad
Established influential madrasa that operated until 1830
Part of scholarly family tradition in Islamic jurisprudence and literature

figures.name: Salemin bin Nasir Al-Suwaidi
figures.title: Chief
figures.roleDescription: Chief of the Sudan tribe (Suwaidi) in Al Bidda
figures.significance: Led the Sudan tribe in Al Bidda and dealt with British authorities during incidents involving pirates and territorial disputes.
figures.biography:
Salemin bin Nasir Al-Suwaidi served as chief of the Sudan tribe (Suwaidi) in Al Bidda during the mid-19th century. In November 1839, when an outlaw from Abu Dhabi named Ghuleta took refuge in Al Bidda, British naval commander A. H. Nott demanded that Al-Suwaidi take Ghuleta into custody and warned him of consequences in case of non-compliance. Al-Suwaidi obliged the British request in February 1840 and also arrested the pirate Jasim bin Jabir and his associates.

Despite his compliance, the British demanded a fine of 300 German krones in compensation for damages incurred by pirates off the coast of Al Bidda. In February 1841, British naval squadrons arrived and threatened consequences if he declined to pay. Al-Suwaidi ultimately declined on the basis that he was uninvolved in bin Jabir's actions. On 26 February, the British fired on Al Bidda, striking a fort and several houses. Al-Suwaidi then paid the fine in full following threats of further action by the British.
achievements: Led the Sudan tribe during British colonial period
Managed tribal relations with British authorities
Maintained tribal leadership during territorial conflicts

figures.name: Sultan bin Nasir Al Suwaidi
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Sheikh of the Sudan tribe and father-in-law of Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan
figures.significance: Connected the Al Suwaidi family to the ruling Al Nahyan family through his daughter's marriage to Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan.
figures.biography:
Sultan bin Nasir Al Suwaidi served as Sheikh of the Sudan tribe during the late 19th century and played a significant role in tribal politics of the region. He became closely connected to the ruling Al Nahyan family when Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan married his daughter, making him the father-in-law of one of Abu Dhabi's most influential rulers.

Sultan bin Nasir was instrumental in Zayed's political schemes, particularly the plan to establish a fort at Al Zorah in Ajman in 1895. It was with the Sudan tribe under Sultan bin Nasir that Zayed conceived this idea to consolidate and build a bridgehead into the northern emirates. In 1897, a section of the Sudan under Sultan bin Nasser requested permission to settle Al Zorah with Zayed's support, which was initially granted by the British Resident, though the scheme was later abandoned due to opposition from other rulers.
achievements: Sheikh of the Sudan tribe
Father-in-law to Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan
Key ally in Abu Dhabi's northern expansion plans

figures.name: Salimayn bin Nasir al-Suwaidi
figures.title: Headman
figures.roleDescription: Headman of the Sudan tribe in Bida'a, Qatar
figures.significance: Led the Sudan tribe in Qatar during conflicts with British forces over piracy accusations.
figures.biography:
Salimayn bin Nasir al-Suwaidi served as headman of the Sudan tribe in Bida'a (present-day Doha) during the early 19th century. In February 1841, he became involved in a significant incident when the Sudan were suspected of harboring the pirate Jasim bin Jabir, known as Raqraqi, in Bida'a. This led to British naval intervention as they sought to punish those they believed were supporting piracy in the Persian Gulf.

When a British squadron arrived to confront him, Salimayn initially resisted their demands. However, after the British fired several shots at the town as a show of force, he was compelled to comply with their ultimatum. He paid a substantial fine in cash and jewelry to the British authorities and handed over Raqraqi's ship, which was subsequently set on fire by the British forces. This incident represents the challenges faced by tribal leaders in Qatar during the period of British maritime dominance in the Gulf.
achievements: Headman of Sudan tribe in Qatar
Navigated tribal leadership during British colonial pressures
Managed resolution of piracy accusations against his tribe

figures.name: Ahmad bin Khalifah Al Suwaidi
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Political leader and opponent in Abu Dhabi succession disputes
figures.significance: Played a key role in Abu Dhabi political affairs and succession disputes in 1928.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Ahmad bin Khalifah Al Suwaidi was a prominent political figure in Abu Dhabi during the early 20th century, particularly influential in matters of succession and tribal politics. He represented the Sudan tribe's interests in the highest levels of Abu Dhabi's political hierarchy and often served as a voice for tribal concerns in the ruling family's deliberations.

In 1928, Ahmad bin Khalifah Al Suwaidi played a crucial role in Abu Dhabi political affairs when he led the opposition to Shakhbut bin Sultan becoming ruler. When this succession was proposed to the family and leading notables, Al Suwaidi organized resistance and instead supported Hazza bin Sultan for the position. His opposition demonstrated the significant political influence that the Sudan tribe, and the Al Suwaidi family in particular, wielded in Abu Dhabi's internal affairs during this critical period of the emirate's development.
achievements: Led opposition in Abu Dhabi succession dispute of 1928
Represented Sudan tribe interests in high-level politics
Influential voice in Abu Dhabi's political decision-making

figures.name: Mansoor Al Yabhouni Al Dhaheri
figures.title: Film Producer and Entrepreneur
figures.roleDescription: Founder and CEO of Film Gate Productions and Al Kalema Productions
figures.significance: He is a prominent Emirati film producer who has contributed significantly to the development of the UAE film industry and received international recognition for his work.
figures.biography:
Mansoor Al Yabhouni Al Dhaheri was born on 14 January 1975 in Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates. He pursued higher education in the United States, earning a Master of Business Administration (MBA) from the New York Institute of Technology (2007–2009) and a Master of Fine Arts (MFA) in Filmmaking from the New York Film Academy (2010–2012). He also received a certificate in Advanced Project Management from Stanford University, demonstrating his commitment to professional development in both business and creative fields.

Al Dhaheri launched Al Kalema Production in January 2012 and Film Gate Production in January 2018, establishing himself as a significant figure in the UAE's growing film industry. His companies have produced a variety of films, TV series, and documentaries that reflect Emirati culture, with productions appearing at prestigious international festivals including the Cannes Film Festival, Dubai International Film Festival, and Abu Dhabi Film Festival. His notable works include 'The Platform' (2020–2021), 'Dreams Drawn by Dust' (2022), 'Scattered Barriers' (2021), 'House of Life' (2022), 'Swim 62' (2023), and 'Abu Dhabi Success Story' (2024). His achievements have been recognized at numerous international festivals, including winning first prize for Best Documentary Film at the Gulf Film Festival in Riyadh (2024) for 'Swim 62', and multiple awards at the New York Film Awards.
achievements: Founded Al Kalema Production (2012) and Film Gate Production (2018)
Won Best Documentary Film at Gulf Film Festival for 'Swim 62' (2024)
Multiple New York Film Awards winner
Productions featured at Cannes Film Festival

figures.name: Mohammed Salem Al Dhaheri
figures.title: Chairman of Al Ain Sports Club
figures.roleDescription: First chairman of Al Ain Sports Club after the 1974 merger
figures.significance: He served as the first chairman of Al Ain Sports Club following the merger with Al Tadhamun Club in 1974.
figures.biography:
Mohammed Salem Al Dhaheri became a significant figure in UAE sports history when he was appointed as the first chairman of the board of directors of Al Ain Sports Club in 1974. This appointment came after the historic merger between Al Ain Club and Al Tadhamun Club on 10 November 1974, which consolidated the football scene in Al Ain city. The merger was an important moment in UAE football, bringing together two clubs that had been formed when some members broke away from the original Al Ain club in 1971.

Under Al Dhaheri's leadership, the newly merged Al Ain Sports Club began its journey toward becoming one of the most successful football clubs in the United Arab Emirates. His role as chairman positioned him at the forefront of the club's early development during a crucial period in UAE football history. The club went on to win its first Abu Dhabi League title in 1974, the same year as the merger, establishing a foundation for future success that would see Al Ain become the most trophy-laden club in UAE football.
achievements: First Chairman of Al Ain Sports Club after 1974 merger
Led the club during its early successful period

figures.name: Nasser al-Dhaheri
figures.title: Writer and Journalist
figures.roleDescription: Emirati writer and journalist
figures.significance: He is a notable Emirati writer and journalist representing the Al Dhaheri family in media and literature.
figures.biography:
Nasser al-Dhaheri is an accomplished Emirati writer and journalist who has contributed to the UAE's literary and media landscape. As a member of the Al Dhaheri family, which traces its heritage to the Dhawahir tribe of the Al Ain region, he represents the intellectual and cultural contributions of the family to modern Emirati society. His work in journalism and writing reflects the growing importance of media and literature in the UAE's cultural development.

His career in journalism positions him as a voice in the UAE's media landscape, contributing to public discourse and cultural expression. The recognition of his work among notable figures from the Al Dhaheri family indicates his significance within the UAE's intellectual community. His contributions to literature and journalism represent the diversification of prominent Emirati families into creative and intellectual fields, moving beyond traditional roles in business and politics.

figures.name: Khaled Abdulla Al Qubaisi
figures.title: CEO and Racing Driver
figures.roleDescription: Former CEO of Real Estate & Infrastructure Investments at Mubadala Investment Company and professional racing driver
figures.significance: He is a prominent Emirati businessman who led major investments at Mubadala and achieved international success in motorsport, including winning the Dubai 24 Hour race twice.
figures.biography:
Khaled Abdulla Al Qubaisi was born on December 22, 1975, and is an accomplished Emirati businessman and racing driver. He holds a Master of Science in Project Management from George Washington University and a bachelor's degree in finance and operations management from Boston University. He was awarded the CFA designation in 2003, demonstrating his financial expertise.

Al Qubaisi served as CEO of Mubadala's Real Estate & Infrastructure Investments platform from 2021 to 2024, overseeing major investment portfolios. Prior to this, he held various senior positions including Chief Human Capital Officer at Mubadala, Chief Investment Officer at International Capital, and Head of Corporate Finance & Business Development at the National Bank of Abu Dhabi. He currently serves as Chairman of the National Central Cooling Company (Tabreed) and sits on numerous corporate boards.

In motorsport, Al Qubaisi has achieved remarkable success in international grand touring races since the early 2010s. His biggest accomplishments include overall victories at the 2012 and 2013 Dubai 24 Hour races, driving a Black Falcon Mercedes-Benz SLS AMG GT3. He also won the Yas Marina 12-Hour Race in 2013 and competed three times in the 24 Hours of Le Mans, with his best finish being 19th overall in 2014. He is married with three children, including two daughters who are also racing drivers.
achievements: CEO of Mubadala Real Estate & Infrastructure Investments (2021-2024)
Overall winner of Dubai 24 Hour race (2012, 2013)

figures.name: Khadem al-Qubaisi
figures.title: Former IPIC Managing Director
figures.roleDescription: Former managing director of International Petroleum Investment Company (IPIC)
figures.significance: He was a senior executive at IPIC who became involved in the 1Malaysia Development Berhad (1MDB) scandal and was arrested in 2016.
figures.biography:
Khadem al-Qubaisi served as the managing director of the International Petroleum Investment Company (IPIC), a major Abu Dhabi-based sovereign wealth fund focused on energy investments. He held a senior position in the UAE's investment landscape and was responsible for managing significant financial portfolios on behalf of the Emirates.

Al-Qubaisi became prominently known due to his involvement in the 1Malaysia Development Berhad (1MDB) scandal, one of the world's largest financial fraud cases. He was arrested in 2016 as authorities investigated the complex web of financial transactions and alleged corruption surrounding the Malaysian sovereign wealth fund. Following the scandal's exposure, IPIC was folded into Aabar Investments as part of restructuring efforts.

According to reports, al-Qubaisi blamed UAE authorities, including Sheikh Mansour bin Zayed Al Nahyan, for using him as a scapegoat in the affair. His case highlighted the complex relationships between sovereign wealth funds, international finance, and the challenges of oversight in large-scale investment operations.
achievements: Managing Director of IPIC
Senior executive in UAE sovereign wealth fund sector

figures.name: Salama bint Butti Al Qubaisi
figures.title: Sheikha
figures.roleDescription: Wife of Abu Dhabi ruler and matriarch of the Al Nahyan ruling dynasty
figures.significance: She was the wife of Sheikh Sultan bin Zayed Al Nahyan and mother of Sheikhs Shakhbut and Zayed, who ruled Abu Dhabi and founded the UAE.
figures.biography:
Sheikha Salama bint Butti Al Qubaisi was from the prominent Al Qubaisi Bedouin tribe from Liwa, who also settled Khor Al Adaid. She was originally from Mezairaa in Liwa. She married Sheikh Sultan bin Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan, who became Ruler of the Emirate of Abu Dhabi from 1922.

After her husband's assassination in 1927, she demonstrated remarkable leadership by taking her son Zayed from Abu Dhabi to Al Ain for safety. She made her sons swear an oath that they would not kill or fight against each other, helping to maintain family unity during a turbulent period. Her sons included Sheikh Shakhbut (who ruled Abu Dhabi from 1928 to 1966), Sheikh Zayed (who ruled Abu Dhabi from 1966 to 2004 and became the first president of the UAE), and Hazza bin Sultan, who served as the Ruler's Representative of the Western Region.

In early 1955, her sons Zayed, Hazza and Shakhbut helped arrange her return to Abu Dhabi on board a Gulf Air plane. She died in October 1970, during the time when her son Zayed was the Ruler of Abu Dhabi. Her legacy is commemorated through the Sheikha Salama Mosque, which was named after her.
achievements: Mother of two Abu Dhabi rulers including UAE founding president Sheikh Zayed
Maintained family unity after husband's assassination
Matriarch of the Al Nahyan ruling dynasty
Commemorated with Sheikha Salama Mosque

figures.roleDescription: Former Commander-in-Chief of Abu Dhabi Police Force
figures.significance: Served as Major General and Commander-in-Chief of Abu Dhabi Police Force from 2016 to 2019, leading one of the UAE's most important security institutions.
figures.biography:
Major General Mohammed Khalfan Al Rumaithi served as the Commander-in-Chief of Abu Dhabi Police Force from 2016 to 2019, during a critical period of modernization and development for the force. His tenure came during the modernization, qualitative and strategic development stage of Abu Dhabi Police, which began in 1995 under Sheikh Saif bin Zayed Al Nahyan's leadership.

As a member of the Al Rumaithi family, his appointment to this senior security position demonstrates the family's trust and standing within UAE security establishments. His leadership of Abu Dhabi Police placed him in charge of one of the most technologically advanced police forces in the region, overseeing approximately 36,000 total staff including civil defence, ambulance and fire services and border security.
achievements: Served as Commander-in-Chief of Abu Dhabi Police Force (2016-2019)
Led major security institution during modernization period

figures.name: Thani Obeid Khamees Al Rumaithi
figures.title: Brigadier, Former Commander of Abu Dhabi Police
figures.roleDescription: Former Commander of Abu Dhabi Police Force
figures.significance: Served as Brigadier and Commander of Abu Dhabi Police from 1991 to 1995, contributing to the force's development during the progressive stage.
figures.biography:
Brigadier Thani Obeid Khamees Al Rumaithi served as Commander of Abu Dhabi Police from 1991 to 1995, during what is known as the progressive stage of the force's development. His tenure came during a crucial period when Abu Dhabi Police was evolving toward eventual affiliation and merging into the Federal Ministry of Interior, following the 1979 merger that made the force part of the federal police and security forces.

His leadership bridged an important transitional period for Abu Dhabi Police, serving just before Sheikh Saif bin Zayed Al Nahyan assumed the role of General Commander in 1995, which marked the beginning of the modernization and strategic development phase of the force.
achievements: Served as Commander of Abu Dhabi Police (1991-1995)
Led force during progressive development stage

figures.name: Ali Mohammed Bakheet Al Rumaithi
figures.title: Board Member, Central Bank of the UAE
figures.roleDescription: Member of the Board of Directors of the Central Bank of the UAE
figures.significance: Serves as a board member of the Central Bank of the UAE, representing the Al Rumaithi family in the country's highest financial institution.
figures.biography:
Ali Mohammed Bakheet Al Rumaithi serves as a member of the Board of Directors of the Central Bank of the United Arab Emirates (CBUAE), the state institution responsible for managing the country's currency, monetary policy, banking and insurance regulation. His appointment to this prestigious position places him among the key decision-makers for the UAE's financial and monetary policies.

As a board member of the CBUAE, Al Rumaithi participates in overseeing the institution's mandate to issue and manage the UAE dirham, ensure currency stability, manage the UAE's credit policy, develop and oversee the banking system, and represent the UAE in international financial institutions such as the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank. His role demonstrates the Al Rumaithi family's involvement in the UAE's financial sector governance.
achievements: Board member of the Central Bank of the UAE
Involved in UAE monetary policy governance

Mohamed Al Mazrouei served as one of the two senior aides of Mohamed bin Zayed (MbZ) and worked as undersecretary at the Abu Dhabi Crown Prince's Court (CPC). He held significant influence in the UAE government structure and was closely involved in the Crown Prince's operations.

Al Mazrouei also served on Manchester City's board during a controversial period when the club was investigated for financial fair play violations. His dual role as a senior government official and football club board member highlighted the close connections between UAE leadership and the club's ownership under Sheikh Mansour bin Zayed Al Nahyan.

figures.name: Mahmoud Pasha Hammadi
figures.title: Pasha and Founder
figures.roleDescription: Founder of Nag Hammadi city and major landholder
figures.significance: Founded the city of Nag Hammadi in Egypt and was a prominent opponent of British colonial rule.
figures.biography:
Mahmoud Pasha Hammadi was a member of the Hammadi family in Sohag, Egypt, who founded the town of Nag Hammadi in the 19th century. The city was established on the site of older villages Ansan and al-Luaqi and was named after its founder. He was a major landholder in Sohag and became known for his strong opposition to British rule in Egypt beginning in 1882.

As a pasha, Mahmoud held significant political and social influence in Upper Egypt. His opposition to British colonial administration made him a notable figure in Egyptian resistance during the early period of British occupation. The city he founded, Nag Hammadi, would later become famous for the discovery of ancient Gnostic texts in 1945, though this occurred long after his time.
achievements: Founded the city of Nag Hammadi
Led opposition to British rule in Egypt from 1882

figures.name: Sa'dun Hammadi
figures.title: Foreign Minister and Prime Minister of Iraq
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi politician, writer, and economist who served in senior government positions
figures.significance: Served as Iraq's Foreign Minister for nearly a decade and briefly as Prime Minister, playing key roles in oil nationalization and international diplomacy.
figures.biography:
Sa'dun Hammadi was born to a Shi'ite family in Karbala on 22 June 1930. He pursued higher education, obtaining a Master's degree in economics from the American University of Beirut and a PhD in economics from the University of Wisconsin–Madison in 1956. Hammadi joined the Ba'ath Party in 1949 and is credited with introducing Ba'athism to Iraq, becoming believed to be the first Iraqi member of the party.

Hammadi's political career began in earnest after the 1963 coup, when he was appointed Minister of Agrarian Reform. He later became head of the Iraq National Oil Company in 1968 and Minister of Oil in 1970, overseeing the historic nationalization of Iraq's oil industry in 1972. From 1974 to 1983, he served as Iraq's Foreign Minister, representing the country during crucial periods including the Iran-Iraq War and conducting diplomatic missions worldwide.

In March 1991, following the Gulf War, Hammadi was appointed Prime Minister by Saddam Hussein but was forced out by September due to his reformist views. He then served as Speaker of the National Assembly from 1996 until the 2003 U.S. invasion. After being detained by American forces for nine months, he was released in 2004 and eventually settled in Qatar, where he engaged in cultural activities until his death from liver cancer in Germany on 14 March 2007.
achievements: Oversaw Iraq's oil nationalization in 1972
Served as Foreign Minister for nearly a decade (1974-1983)
Introduced Ba'athism to Iraq as first Iraqi party member
Authored numerous cultural and political works

figures.name: Juma Mohammed Al Kaabi
figures.title: UAE Ambassador to Afghanistan
figures.roleDescription: Emirati career diplomat who served as UAE Ambassador to Afghanistan
figures.significance: Emirati diplomat who died from injuries sustained in the 2017 Afghanistan bombings while on a humanitarian mission.
figures.biography:
Juma Mohammed Al Kaabi was born in 1966 in the village of Wadi Al Qor in Ras Al Khaimah, United Arab Emirates. He was the eldest of his siblings, having 2 brothers and 6 sisters. His father died when he was 18 years old, and he became the primary caretaker of his family. After finishing high school, he joined the United Arab Emirates Armed Forces, then worked for the UAE Ministry of Foreign Affairs for 8 years before being appointed as the UAE Ambassador to Afghanistan on July 28, 2016.

On January 10, 2017, Al Kaabi was on a humanitarian mission to Kandahar province, Afghanistan, to lay the foundation stone of an orphanage sponsored by the UAE government. A bombing occurred while he was at a dinner visit at the Kandahar governor's house, killing 13 people and injuring 18 others, including Al Kaabi. He was evacuated to Abu Dhabi and then transferred to a specialized hospital in France for treatment. On February 15, 2017, the Ministry of Presidential Affairs announced that Al Kaabi had died due to his injuries. He was married and had 3 daughters, 2 sons, and 2 grandchildren.
achievements: Served as UAE Ambassador to Afghanistan
Died while on humanitarian mission to open an orphanage

figures.name: Saad Sherida al-Kaabi
figures.title: Qatar Energy Minister
figures.roleDescription: Current Qatar Energy Minister who succeeded Mohammed Saleh Al Sada
figures.significance: Current Qatar Energy Minister who has played a key role in Qatar's energy sector and made significant statements during the 2026 Iran war regarding global energy security.
figures.biography:
Saad Sherida al-Kaabi serves as Qatar's Energy Minister, having succeeded Mohammed Saleh Al Sada in this role. He has been instrumental in overseeing Qatar's vast energy resources and managing the country's position as a major global supplier of liquefied natural gas. During the 2026 Iran war, al-Kaabi made significant statements about the potential global economic impact of the conflict on energy markets.

In March 2026, during the Iran conflict, al-Kaabi warned that if the war continued, other Gulf energy producers might be forced to halt exports and declare force majeure, stating that 'this will bring down economies of the world.' His warnings came after QatarEnergy announced it was declaring Force Majeure on its contracts with buyers following reported drone attacks on Qatari gas facilities. Al-Kaabi's statements highlighted the critical importance of Gulf energy exports to the global economy and the potential catastrophic consequences of prolonged regional conflicts on worldwide energy security.
achievements: Current Qatar Energy Minister
Made significant statements on global energy security during regional conflicts
