figures.biography:
Ahmad Majid Al Ghurair was Juma al Majid's uncle and an established merchant in Dubai's Deira district. When Juma turned 15 years old, Ahmad asked him to assist at his shop in Deira, providing the young man with his first business experience. Recognizing Juma's potential, Ahmad eventually opened a second small shop for his nephew, which became the foundation for what would later grow into the Juma Al Majid business empire.

Ahmad's mentorship was instrumental in Juma's early commercial development, teaching him the fundamentals of trade and business operations in Dubai's emerging economy. This early guidance and opportunity provided by Ahmad helped establish the business acumen that would later make Juma one of the UAE's most successful entrepreneurs.

figures.name: Mohammad Al Qaz
figures.title: Business Partner and Co-founder
figures.roleDescription: Financier, developer, diplomat, and philanthropist
figures.significance: Co-founded the Mohamed & Juma Al Majid company and served as first Deputy Chairman of the UAE Central Bank.
figures.biography:
Mohamed Abdallah AlGaz was born in Al Dhagaya, Deira in 1920 to a modest family. After his father died when he was young, he was educated at Al Ahmadiya School. During World War II, he worked as a carpenter in Dammam and started a commodity trading operation. He became a trader in textiles and commodities between Middle Eastern markets and entered the historic Switzerland-Dubai-India gold trade in the 1950s after partnering with Juma Al Majid.

AlGaz rose to political prominence as a trusted advisor to Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed al Maktoum and was nominated to the Dubai Municipal Council in 1958. He served as a diplomatic channel between Dubai and Abu Dhabi during land negotiations. After the Indian gold trade declined in the early 1970s, AlGaz and Al Majid invested in franchise rights for international brands including General Electric, Samsung, Pepsi, Nissan, Hyundai, and Kia. After 11 years of partnership, they amicably split their assets, with AlGaz focusing on real estate development.

Post-federation, AlGaz served as the first Deputy Chairman of the Central Bank of the UAE from 1980 into the mid-1990s under Chairman Sheikh Suroor bin Mohammad Al Nahyan. He was instrumental in establishing educational institutions and co-founded the UAE's first charitable society, Beit Al Khair, in 1989 with Juma Al Majid. He died in 2016 at age 96.

Hana bint Juma Al Majid is a member of the prominent Juma Al Majid merchant family who married into the Al Qasimi ruling family of Ras Al Khaimah. She became the mother of Sheikh Mohammed bin Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi, who was born on February 9, 1987, and later became Crown Prince of Ras Al Khaimah.

Her marriage represents an important alliance between one of the UAE's most successful merchant families and the ruling family of Ras Al Khaimah, demonstrating the interconnected nature of prominent Emirati families across the federation.

The bank initially began as a group of banking and commercial operations which, in 1978, joined together under the umbrella of the Alrajhi Trading and Exchange Company. The company changed to a joint stock company in 1987, and after two years was rebranded as the Alrajhi Banking and Investment Corporation, before becoming Alrajhi Bank in 2006.

The U.S. Supreme Court issued an order on June 30, 2014, leaving intact the dismissal with prejudice of all claims against the bank and its officers, including Abdullah bin Suleiman Alrajhi, related to the terrorist attacks of September 11, 2001.

figures.title: Founder of The Olayan Group
figures.roleDescription: Entrepreneur and founder of multinational business empire
figures.significance: Founded The Olayan Group in 1947, which became a major multinational enterprise with operations worth over $10 billion.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Sulaiman Al-Olayan was a Saudi entrepreneur who founded The Olayan Group in 1947, establishing what would become one of the region's most successful business empires. The group grew into a private multinational enterprise engaged in distribution, manufacturing, services and investments, with operations spanning the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and the Middle East. Under his leadership, the company developed partnerships with leading multinationals and became one of the largest investors in Saudi and regional stock markets.

Olayan's business acumen and vision transformed the company from its modest beginnings into a diversified conglomerate. By the time of his death, the private family fortune was estimated to top $10 billion. His legacy continued through his children, particularly his daughter Lubna, who became CEO of Olayan Financing Company. The Olayan Group's influence extended beyond business, with significant investments in education, infrastructure, and various sectors across the Middle East.
achievements: Founded The Olayan Group in 1947
Built multinational enterprise worth over $10 billion
Established partnerships with leading international companies
Created one of the largest investment portfolios in Saudi Arabia and the region

figures.name: Lubna Suliman al-Olayan
figures.title: Former CEO of Olayan Financing Company
figures.roleDescription: Prominent Saudi businesswoman and banking executive
figures.significance: First Saudi woman to head a bank and former CEO of Olayan Financing Company, one of the most powerful women in global business.
figures.biography:
Lubna Suliman al-Olayan was born on August 4, 1955, in Khobar, in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, as the youngest child of Sheikh Sulaiman Al-Olayan, founder of The Olayan Group. She obtained a Bachelor of Science in Agriculture from Cornell University and a Master's degree in Business Administration from Indiana University. After working at Morgan Guaranty Trust Bank in New York City from 1979 to 1981, she returned to Saudi Arabia to join the family business.

She served as CEO of Olayan Financing Company (OFC) until May 2019, overseeing the holding entity for The Olayan Group's operations in Saudi Arabia and the Middle East. Under her leadership, OFC operated or participated in more than 40 companies and became one of the largest investors in Saudi and regional stock markets. In June 2019, she made history by becoming the first Saudi woman to head a bank when she was named Chairwoman of the Saudi British Bank (SABB), a position she has held through multiple re-elections.

Beyond her corporate role, Lubna has served on numerous prestigious boards including WPP, Citigroup's International Advisory Board, and Rolls-Royce. She has been a trustee of Cornell University, a board member of INSEAD, and serves on the Board of Trustees of the World Economic Forum. Forbes has consistently ranked her among the world's most powerful women, listing her as the 86th most powerful woman globally as of 2014.
achievements: First Saudi woman to head a bank (SABB)

As a board member, Khaled plays a role in overseeing the group's diverse operations across distribution, manufacturing, services, and investments. The Olayan family's collective involvement in the business has helped maintain the company's position as one of the region's most successful private enterprises, with the family fortune estimated to exceed $10 billion.

figures.name: Osama bin Laden
figures.title: Terrorist Leader
figures.roleDescription: Founder and leader of al-Qaeda terrorist organization
figures.significance: Co-founded the terrorism group Al-Qaeda and was responsible for attacks including the September 11 attacks.
figures.biography:
Osama bin Laden was born in 1957 in Saudi Arabia, the 17th child of Muhammad bin Awad bin Laden and the only son of his tenth wife, Hamida al-Attas, who was of Syrian origin. His parents divorced soon after his birth, and his mother was given in marriage to one of his father's company executives around 1958. Osama was raised in the household of his stepfather Muhammad al-Attas in Jeddah. In 1994, the bin Laden family publicly disowned him, and the Saudi government revoked his passport and citizenship for speaking out against the government for permitting U.S. troops to be based in Saudi Arabia.

Osama founded al-Qaeda and became responsible for numerous terrorist attacks, most notably the September 11, 2001 attacks on the United States. He lived in exile in Sudan and Afghanistan before ultimately hiding in a compound in Abbottabad, Pakistan. He was killed on May 2, 2011, during a U.S. Navy SEAL operation at his compound. He had multiple wives and over 20 children, and was one of the FBI's Most Wanted Terrorists until his death.

figures.name: Muhammad bin Awad bin Laden
figures.title: Founder and Patriarch
figures.roleDescription: Founder of the bin Laden family fortune and construction empire
figures.significance: Founded what is today the Saudi Binladin Group and became the wealthiest non-royal Saudi, establishing the wealth and prestige of the bin Laden family.
figures.biography:
Muhammad bin Awad bin Laden was born around 1908 on the Hadramawt coast of south Yemen to Awad, a Kindite Hadrami tribesman from al-Rubat, a village in Wadi Doan. Poor and uneducated, he emigrated to Saudi Arabia before World War I, initially working as a porter in Jeddah. In 1931, he started his own construction business and came to the attention of Abdul Aziz Ibn Saud through construction projects. He made his initial fortune from exclusive rights to construct all mosques and other religious buildings not only in Saudi Arabia, but as far as Ibn Saud's influence reached.

Muhammad strategically built strong ties with the Saudi royal family, notably constructing a ramp at the palace in Jeddah to help the aging king move between floors. In 1964, King Faisal issued a royal decree awarding all future construction projects to Muhammad's company, helping the company amass assets exceeding $5 billion. He fathered 54 children by 22 wives, following Islamic polygyny principles. On September 3, 1967, Muhammad was killed when his airplane crashed during landing in Usran, 'Asir Province. His funeral drew nearly 10,000 people, and King Faisal placed his children under royal protection.
achievements: Founded the Saudi Binladin Group construction empire
Secured exclusive rights to construct all mosques in Saudi Arabia
Built strong ties with Saudi royal family through major construction projects
Amassed company assets exceeding $5 billion

figures.name: Omar bin Laden
figures.title: Artist and Cultural Ambassador
figures.roleDescription: Saudi artist, author, and businessman who publicly disavowed his father's actions
figures.significance: The only son of Osama bin Laden to publicly disavow his father's actions and became an advocate for peace.
figures.biography:
Omar bin Laden was born on March 1, 1981, as the fourth-eldest son of Osama bin Laden and his first wife Najwa Ghanhem. He accompanied his father during exile to Sudan and then to Afghanistan, and trained in al-Qaeda camps beginning at age 14. After six years of training and sharing a house with al-Qaeda's second-in-command Ayman al-Zawahiri, Omar left al-Qaeda in 2000 because he disagreed with killing civilians, with his father not objecting to his departure.

Omar returned to Saudi Arabia and ran his own company in Jeddah as a contractor and scrap metal dealer. In 2006, he married Jane Felix-Browne (Zaina Mohamed Al-Sabah), a British parish councillor 24 years his senior. He has been denied visas to the UK and Europe, and has sought asylum in various countries. Omar co-authored the book 'Growing Up bin Laden' with his mother in 2009, detailing their harsh life under Osama's control. He has consistently advocated for peace and condemned his father's terrorist activities while expressing personal love for him as his father.
achievements: Co-authored 'Growing Up bin Laden' memoir
Became advocate for peace between Muslims and the West
Only son of Osama bin Laden to publicly disavow father's actions

figures.name: Hamza bin Laden
figures.title: Al-Qaeda Member
figures.roleDescription: Key member of al-Qaeda and son of Osama bin Laden
figures.significance: Was considered a key leader of Al-Qaeda and was being groomed as his father's potential successor before being killed in a U.S. counterterrorism operation.
figures.biography:
Hamza bin Laden was born on May 9, 1989, in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, the son of Osama bin Laden and Khairiah Sabir. In January 2001, he attended his brother Mohammed's wedding in Kandahar, Afghanistan, and video footage from November 2001 shows 12-year-old Hamza handling U.S. helicopter wreckage alongside the Taliban. From March 2002 to July or August 2010, Hamza was detained in Iran under prison-like conditions during his adolescence, from age 12 to 21.

After his release, Hamza moved to Qatar for several years to study Islamic religious texts as his father had recommended, then returned to Pakistan around 2015. Between 2015 and 2017, he released audio and video messages calling for attacks against Western targets. The U.S. classified him as a Specially Designated Global Terrorist in January 2017 and offered a $1 million reward for information on his whereabouts in February 2019. Saudi Arabia revoked his citizenship in March 2019. On September 14, 2019, President Donald Trump confirmed that Hamza had been killed in a U.S. counterterrorism operation in the Afghanistan-Pakistan region, though some 2024 reports claim he may still be alive.

figures.name: Yeslam bin Laden
figures.title: Business Executive
figures.roleDescription: Geneva-based head of family's European operations
figures.significance: Head of the family's European holding company and became a Swiss citizen, representing the international expansion of bin Laden business interests.
figures.biography:
Yeslam bin Laden was born in 1950 and studied in the 1970s at the University of Southern California in Los Angeles. He later settled in Switzerland and became a Swiss citizen in 2001. Yeslam serves as the Geneva-based head of the family's European holding company, the Saudi Investment Company, representing the bin Laden family's international business interests beyond the Middle East.

Yeslam was scrutinized by Swiss and American investigators because of a financial stake he held in a Swiss aviation firm. He has claimed to not have had contact with his half-brother Osama since 1981, distancing himself from Osama's terrorist activities. Yeslam married Carmen Dufour and has three daughters: Wafah Dufour (born 1975), who became an American model and aspiring singer-songwriter, Najia Dufour (born 1981), and Noor Dufour (born 1987). The family lived in Geneva, and after Yeslam and Carmen separated in 1988, the children remained primarily in Switzerland.
achievements: Established European operations for bin Laden business interests
Obtained Swiss citizenship while maintaining family business ties
Studied at University of Southern California

figures.name: Abdullah bin Osama bin Laden
figures.title: Businessman
figures.roleDescription: Eldest son of Osama bin Laden who runs advertising firm in Jeddah
figures.significance: The eldest son of Osama bin Laden who completely broke with his father and established his own business in Saudi Arabia.
figures.biography:
Abdullah bin Osama bin Laden was born in 1976 as the eldest son of Osama bin Laden and Najwa Ghanem. Although his father initially showed him favor, taking him to Afghanistan in 1984 when he was only eight, Abdullah later became disgusted with life in Sudan. He moved back to Jeddah to marry his first cousin and refused to rejoin his father in Sudan, except for a brief, unpleasant visit. Abdullah never saw or communicated with his father again after 1995.

Abdullah now resides in Jeddah where he runs his own firm called Fame Advertising. He has been closely watched by the Saudi government, which restricted his travel from the kingdom after 1996, at least for a time. He has stated that the media have misrepresented his father and is known to dine occasionally with his uncle, Saudi Binladin Group chairman Bakr bin Laden, at the Intercontinental Hotel in Jeddah. In 2015, WikiLeaks revealed that Abdullah had requested his father's death certificate from the U.S. embassy in Saudi Arabia, but was told that no death certificate was issued for Osama.

Successfully distanced himself from father's terrorist activities
Maintained business operations despite family scrutiny

Along with his brothers Abdulaziz and Sulaiman, he expanded the company's operations significantly from the original trading business to include diverse sectors. The company grew to encompass Pepsi Cola bottling franchises, oilfield services for Saudi Aramco, real estate, hospitality, food & beverages, and investments in banking, insurance, shipping, trading, finance, and manufacturing.

Ahmad died in 1991, and his equity in the general partnership was transferred to his sons Yousef Ahmad Al Gosaibi, Abdulmohsin Ahmad Al Gosaibi, Khalid Ahmad Al Gosaibi, and three of his daughters. Each of the three founding brothers owned one third of AHAB, making the Al Gosaibi Group a significant family-owned business empire in the Middle East.

Abdullah Al-Hamad Al Zamil was originally from Onaiza, Central Arabia, and established a food and textile trading entity in Bahrain in the 1930s. He later expanded his business interests into real estate investment. He was the father of twelve sons, including Abdulaziz bin Abdullah Al Zamil who would later become a prominent industrialist and government minister.

Upon his death in 1961, the family chose to continue operation of his business as a group under the leadership of his eldest son, Mohammed. This decision laid the foundation for what would become the Zamil Group Holding Company, which now employs more than 12,000 people in 60 countries and is involved in diverse sectors including general construction, paints, plastics, petrochemicals, shipbuilding, and port management.

figures.biography:
Sheikh Abdullah Al Turki served as secretary-general of the World Muslim League and was a member of the Council of Senior Ulema. He was directly challenged by Prince Turki bin Faisal Al Saud after the 9/11 attacks regarding the proper role of religious scholars versus rulers in matters of state.

Prince Turki bin Faisal argued against Sheikh Al Turki's position, stating that 'those responsible for affairs of state are the rulers,' whereas religious scholars 'only act in an advisory capacity.' This public disagreement highlighted tensions within Saudi religious and political establishments following the September 11 attacks.

figures.biography:
Ahmed Al Fardan is a Bahraini former rally driver who competed in national and regional rallying championships across the Middle East. He was a regular participant in the Bahrain National Rally Championship and events organized under the Bahrain Motor Federation (BMF). His active racing years coincided with the growth of international motorsport across the Middle East, where he participated in regional events such as the Bahrain International Rally and other rounds of the FIA Middle East Rally Championship.

Following his retirement from active competition, Al Fardan transitioned into a managerial and advisory role, becoming instrumental in managing the career of his son, Hamad Al Fardan, guiding him through various international open-wheel categories including Formula BMW Asia, British Formula 3, and the GP2 Asia Series. He was also known for his involvement with Gulf Finance House (GFH) racing initiatives, which sponsored Bahraini drivers on the international stage to bridge the gap between regional racing and global platforms like Formula 1.

figures.name: Hamad Al Fardan

figures.significance: First Bahraini racing driver to compete at GP2 Series level, pioneering Bahraini participation in international motorsport.
figures.biography:
Hamad Al Fardan was born on June 29, 1987, in Manama, Bahrain, as the son of rally driver Ahmed Al Fardan. He began his formula racing career in 2004 with the Formula BMW Asia series, where he achieved significant success. In 2005, he completed a full season with the Malaysian team Meritus and finished third overall, also competing in the Formula BMW World Final. His career progressed through various international series including Formula Renault, Toyota Racing Series in New Zealand, and British Formula 3.

Al Fardan made history in 2008 when he signed with iSport International to compete in the GP2 Asia Series, becoming the first Bahraini and Arabian driver to race at this level. His participation was backed by Gulf Finance House, which led to the team being renamed GFH Team iSport for the duration of the championship. Throughout his career, he competed across multiple continents, from Asia to Europe to New Zealand, winning races in Formula BMW Asia, Formula Asia V6 Renault, and the New Zealand Grand Prix. His achievements helped establish Bahrain's presence in international motorsport before the country hosted its first Formula 1 Grand Prix in 2004.

figures.biography:
Sheikha Moza bint Nasser Al-Missned was born on August 8, 1959, daughter of Nasser bin Abdullah Al-Missned, a well-known opposition activist and former head of the Al-Muhannada confederation of Bani Hajer. She spent much of her childhood in Kuwait during her father's exile following his imprisonment for political activities against the policies of the deposed emir Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani. She married Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, the heir apparent of Qatar, in 1977, the same year her father returned to Qatar. She is one of his three wives and the mother of seven children, including the current Emir Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani.

Sheikha Moza received a BA in Sociology from Qatar University in 1986 and holds an MA in Public Policy in Islam from Hamad Bin Khalifa University. She has received honorary doctorates from prestigious institutions including Virginia Commonwealth University, Texas A&M, Carnegie Mellon, Imperial College London, and Georgetown University. She co-founded the Qatar Foundation for Education, Science and Community Development in 1995 and has been instrumental in establishing Education City in Doha, bringing Western universities to Qatar as part of the country's soft power strategy.

She has held numerous influential positions including UNESCO's Special Envoy for Basic and Higher Education (2003-2023), chairperson of Silatech since 2008, and founder of the Supreme Council for Family Affairs in 1998. She established Education Above All in 2012, focusing on making education accessible to marginalized children globally. She has been recognized as one of the '100 Most Powerful Women' by Forbes and has significant political influence in shaping Qatar's international relations and domestic policies.

Shaikha Rodha Al-Misnad is married to Sheikh Fahad bin Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, who is the second son of former Emir Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani. Through this marriage, she connects the Al Misnad merchant family with the ruling Al Thani family, representing an important alliance between prominent Qatari families.

She and Sheikh Fahad have four children together: one son, Shaikh Hamad bin Fahad Al-Hamad Al-Thani, and three daughters: Shaikha Mariam, Shaikha Asma, and Shaikha Nayla bint Fahad Al-Hamad Al-Thani. He became notable in legal proceedings when he, along with Omar Ali Babtain, lodged a challenge against Adel Flaifel with the Supreme Court in Brisbane, Australia. They claimed that Flaifel had sold them properties in the Middle East worth 59.5 million Australian dollars but never transferred the contracts, and had used that money to purchase Australian properties. This case highlighted the Al Misnad family's international business dealings and their involvement in significant commercial transactions across different countries.

figures.biography:
Mishal Hamed Kanoo was born on February 25, 1969, in Dubai and educated through high school locally before continuing college studies in the United States. He earned a double major in Comparative Theology and Philosophy and Economics as his first degree, later obtaining his MBA in Finance from the University of St. Thomas in Houston. After leaving the business temporarily, he earned a second MBA at the American University of Sharjah, where he subsequently became a visiting lecturer on Family Business.

Kanoo worked at Arthur Andersen in Dubai as an auditor before returning to The Kanoo Group as Deputy Chairman in 1997, serving under his uncle Mubarak Kanoo who was then chairman of the holding company. In 2015, he assumed the Chairmanship of The Kanoo Group following the passing of Yusuf Ahmed Kanoo in 2014. Under his leadership, the company operates across diverse sectors including shipping, travel, machinery, logistics, property, energy, chemicals, training, retail, and various joint ventures.

Beyond the family business, Kanoo holds chairman or director positions in multiple companies including Dubai Express LLC/Freightworks, Johnson Arabia LLC, Wolffkran Arabia LLC, and Gulf Capital Pvt. JSC. He is actively involved in Dubai's art scene, co-founding the Meem Gallery in 2007 with Sultan Sooud Al-Qassemi and Charles Pocock. The gallery specializes in modern and contemporary Arab and Iranian art and houses the Noor Library of Middle Eastern Art, one of the largest international resource centers for Middle Eastern arts.

figures.name: Abdul Latif Jassim Kanoo
figures.title: Founder
figures.roleDescription: Founder of Beit Al Quran museum and collector of Islamic art
figures.significance: Founded the Beit Al Quran in 1990, which became one of the most renowned Islamic museums in the world.
figures.biography:
Abdul Latif Jassim Kanoo was an avid collector of Quranic manuscripts and Islamic art who felt a strong sense of responsibility toward the rare manuscripts he had acquired over the years. As his collection grew, he came to feel that these historic artifacts required proper preservation and public access. This led him to conceive of establishing a first-of-its-kind institution dedicated to the service of the Quran and preservation of historic manuscripts.

Construction of the Beit Al Quran complex began in 1984, and Kanoo officially opened the museum in March 1990. The concept of a comprehensive institution housing Quranic manuscripts and Islamic art was unique in the Persian Gulf region at the time. Kanoo donated his entire personal collection of Quranic manuscripts and Islamic art to form the core of the museum's holdings, which represents various calligraphic traditions from the first Hijri century to the present day.

The establishment was funded completely by public donations, with contributions from people across all walks of life in Bahrain, from heads of state to school children. The complex includes not only the museum but also a mosque, library, auditorium, and madrasa. The Beit Al Quran houses an internationally celebrated collection of historic Quranic manuscripts from across the Islamic world, from China in the East to Spain in the West, making it one of the most important repositories of Islamic manuscripts globally.
achievements: Founded Beit Al Quran museum in 1990

figures.biography:
Ahmed bin Mohamed Kanoo was the patriarch who established the foundational trading business that would eventually grow into the Yusuf Bin Ahmed Kanoo Group. He operated a small foodstuffs trading company in Bahrain, where his son Yusuf bin Ahmed Kanoo worked from an early age, learning the fundamentals of trade and commerce.

When Yusuf took over the business from his father in 1890 at the age of 22, Ahmed's small trading operation served as the launching point for what would become one of the largest independent trading companies in the Gulf region. The business foundation that Ahmed established in foodstuffs trading provided the platform for Yusuf's subsequent expansion into shipping, importing commodities from Kerala and Mombasa, and developing extensive trade networks across the Indian coast and East African ports.

figures.biography:
Sarah Ali Kanoo was a member of the extended Kanoo family who married her cousin Abdulaziz Jassim Kanoo, strengthening the family bonds within the merchant dynasty. Together, they raised four children: Ali, Saud, Nawf, and Badr, who represent the next generation of the Kanoo family.

As the wife of one of the most prominent businessmen in the Gulf region and a matriarch of the Kanoo family, Sarah played an important role in maintaining family unity and traditions. She survived her husband Abdulaziz, who died in August 2016, but passed away herself on January 8, 2018. She was buried in Manama Cemetery, joining other members of the Kanoo family who had contributed to Bahrain's commercial and social development over more than a century.

figures.biography:
Abdullatif bin Rashid Al Zayani was born on 15 April 1954 in Muharraq, Kingdom of Bahrain. He graduated from the Royal Military Academy Sandhurst in the United Kingdom and received advanced degrees including a PhD in operations research from the Naval Postgraduate School, Monterey, California in 1986. He became a commissioned officer in the Bahrain Defence Force in 1973, rising through the ranks to lieutenant general in 2010 before his military career ended on 2 June 2004.

Zayani served as Secretary General of the Gulf Cooperation Council from 1 April 2011 to February 2020, becoming the fifth GCC secretary general and the first with military background since the GCC was established. In January 2020, he was appointed as Bahrain's Foreign Minister, replacing Khalid bin Ahmed Al Khalifa. On 15 September 2020, Zayani signed the official Bahrain–Israel normalization agreement at the White House in Washington, D.C. In May 2023, he made history by becoming the first Bahraini foreign minister to visit Ukraine since diplomatic relations were established in 1992.

figures.name: Muhammad ibn Ali al-Jawad
figures.title: Ninth Imam of Twelver Shia
figures.roleDescription: Religious leader and descendant of Prophet Muhammad
figures.significance: Ninth of the Twelve Imams in Shia Islam, known for his religious teachings and correspondence with followers on Islamic law.
figures.biography: Muhammad ibn Ali al-Jawad was born in Medina around 8 April 811 as the son of Ali al-Rida, the eighth Imam. He became Imam at the young age of seven after his father's death in 818, which initially caused controversy among Shia communities due to his youth. Despite this, most Imamite Shias accepted his imamate, believing he received perfect religious knowledge through divine inspiration. In 830, he was summoned to Baghdad by Caliph al-Ma'mun and married to the caliph's daughter Umm al-Fadl, though this marriage remained childless. He organized Shia community affairs through an extensive network of representatives (wokala) across the Abbasid empire. In 835, he was again summoned to Baghdad by Caliph al-Mu'tasim and died there at age 25. Shia sources unanimously claim he was poisoned by his wife at the instigation of al-Mu'tasim, while Sunni sources remain silent about the cause of his death.
achievements: Maintained extensive network of representatives across Abbasid empire
Preserved numerous religious teachings and correspondence on Islamic law
Successfully consolidated imamate despite young age

figures.name: Ali ibn Muhammad al-Hadi
figures.title: Tenth Imam of Twelver Shia
figures.roleDescription: Religious leader and son of Muhammad al-Jawad
figures.significance: Tenth of the Twelve Imams who was held under surveillance in Samarra for twenty years, marking the end of direct Imam leadership of the Shia community.
figures.biography: Ali ibn Muhammad al-Hadi was born on 7 March 828 in Sorayya, a village near Medina, as the son of Muhammad al-Jawad and Samana, a freed slave of North African origin. He became Imam at age seven after his father's death in 835. Around 848, he was summoned from Medina to the Abbasid capital Samarra by Caliph al-Mutawakkil, known for his anti-Shia policies. There he lived under constant surveillance for twenty years until his death, unable to freely meet with ordinary Shias. Despite restrictions, he maintained communication with an underground network of representatives who organized Shia community affairs. He endured numerous attempts by Abbasid court members to humiliate him, house searches, temporary imprisonments, and alleged murder plots. He died on 21 June 868 during the caliphate of al-Mu'tazz, with most Shia sources claiming he was poisoned by the Abbasids. His restricted life in Samarra effectively ended the direct leadership of the Shia community by the Imams.
achievements: Maintained underground network of representatives despite surveillance
Endured twenty years of persecution while preserving Shia community organization
Authored theological treatise on free will and various religious texts

figures.name: Sheikh Mirza Jawad Kubar Tabrizi
figures.title: Grand Ayatollah
figures.roleDescription: Iranian Shia marja and religious authority
figures.significance: Prominent Iranian Shia religious authority and student of Grand Ayatollah al-Khoei, known for his scholarship and mourning rituals advocacy.
figures.biography: Sheikh Mirza Jawad Kubar Tabrizi was born in Tabriz in 1926 to Ali Kubar, a merchant, and Fatima Sultan, claiming descent from Prophet Muhammad through his mother. He initially pursued secular education but changed to Islamic seminary studies despite family opposition due to Pahlavi government pressure on clerics. He traveled to Qom in 1948 to study under Grand Ayatollah Borujerdi, then moved to Najaf in 1953 where he became a close student of Sayyid Abu Al-Qasim al-Khoei for nearly twenty years. He obtained the level of ijtihad and specialized in philosophy, Quranic exegesis, and rijal. In 1976, he was deported by the Baathist regime and returned to Iran, settling in Qom where he resumed teaching. After al-Khoei's death in 1992, many followers turned to him as their marja. He was known for his passionate advocacy of mourning rituals for Imam Husayn and his strong positions on religious matters. He died on November 20, 2006, and was buried in the Fatima Masumeh Shrine in Qom.
achievements: Became recognized marja after al-Khoei's death
Authored numerous volumes on jurisprudence and principles
Led large annual mourning processions in Qom
Issued statements on international religious freedom issues

figures.name: Jawad Ahmad
figures.title: Pakistani Pop Singer and Politician
figures.roleDescription: Musician turned politician and social activist
figures.significance: Pakistani pop singer who transitioned to politics and founded the leftist Barabri Party Pakistan while maintaining social work initiatives.
figures.biography: Jawad Ahmad was born on 29 September 1970 in Lahore into a Kashmiri family, with both parents being college professors teaching Political Science. He received a degree in Mechanical Engineering from the University of Engineering and Technology (UET), Lahore, where he participated in musical and literary societies. While at university, he became a member of the pop band Jupiters alongside Ali Azmat, before launching a solo career. He gained fame with his song 'Allah Meray Dil Kay Ander,' showcasing his Sufist inclinations, and released three solo albums plus several drama OSTs. In 2002, he was appointed as Pakistan's ambassador for polio eradication by the Ministry of Health and UNICEF. In May 2017, he launched his leftist Barabri political party, fielding 14 candidates in the 2018 elections though winning no seats. He runs ten schools in Pakistan's least developed areas under the Taleem for All program and continues his social work alongside his political activities.
achievements: Released three successful solo albums
Appointed Pakistan's polio eradication ambassador
Founded Barabri political party
Established ten schools for underprivileged areas
Created awareness campaigns against drug addiction

figures.name: Allama Syed Jawad Naqvi
figures.title: Islamic Scholar and Religious Leader
figures.roleDescription: Pakistani Shia theorist and founder of Islamic educational institutions
figures.significance: Leading Pakistani Shia theorist who founded multiple Islamic seminaries and promotes theories of Wilayat-i Faqih, often called the representative of the Wilayat in Pakistan.
figures.biography: Allama Syed Jawad Naqvi was born on 1 February 1963 in Thipra Syedan village in Haripur Hazara District, Pakistan. He studied religious philosophy, theology, and Shia jurisprudence in Islamic seminaries in Pakistan and Iran, with notable teachers including Abdollah Javadi-Amoli and Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah-Yazdi. He founded and serves as chancellor of Jamia Urwat-ul-Wusqa in Lahore and many other Islamic schools, including Jamia Jaffria in Gujranwala and Jamia Ummul Kitaab in Lahore. He also heads the Deen-ul-Qayyim Online Islamic school and Siraat Education School System. Naqvi is the editor of the monthly religious magazine Masharab-e-Naab and is known for his dialogue-filled sermons. He is a staunch supporter of Iran's Islamic Revolution and propagates hard-line versions of Wilayat-i Faqih. In 2012-2013, he led the Islamic Awakening Movement condemning blasphemy of Muhammad. He has authored several works in Urdu on Islamic topics and continues to be a influential voice in Pakistani Shia communities.
achievements: Founded multiple Islamic seminaries including Jamia Urwat-ul-Wusqa
Established monthly magazine Masharab-e-Naab
Led Islamic Awakening Movement in 2012-2013
Authored several books on Islam including 'Islam and Secularism'

figures.name: Abdullatif Muhammad Thunayyan Al-Ghanim
figures.title: First Chairman of the Kuwait Constituent Assembly
figures.roleDescription: Founding political figure and constitutional architect
figures.significance: Served as the first Chairman of the Kuwait Constituent Assembly and led the drafting of Kuwait's Constitution.
figures.biography:
Abdullatif Muhammad Thunayyan Al-Ghanim was born in 1912 in the Qibla district of Kuwait City to a wealthy merchant family. He attended Al-Mubarakiya School and then Al-Ahmadiya School before traveling to India with his father in 1922, where his father ran a business in Karachi and Al-Ghanim learned English. His work in the family business led to extensive travel between Kuwait, India, and Iraq, giving him exposure to various cultural and political influences.

Al-Ghanim's political career began early when he was elected to the first Legislative Council following the June 1938 Kuwaiti general election, and again to the second Legislative Council in 1939. In 1952, he won election to the Municipal Council of Kuwait and became director of his municipality. His most significant contribution came in 1962 when he was elected to the Constituent National Assembly and was unanimously named chairman. As one of five members of the Constitutional Drafting Committee, he chaired the committee that created Kuwait's current Constitution.

Following his constitutional work, Al-Ghanim was nominated by Emir Abdullah Al-Salim Al-Sabah to serve as Minister of Public Health in January 1963, and later as Minister of Public Works in January 1964. He died on March 14, 1988, and was remembered as 'one of Kuwait's most loyal men, who contributed to laying the foundations of the country's political history.'
achievements: First Chairman of the Kuwait Constituent Assembly (1962-1963)
Led drafting of Kuwait's Constitution
Minister of Public Health (1963)
Minister of Public Works (1964)

figures.biography: Mohammed Abdulmohsen al-Kharafi was the patriarch of the Al-Kharafi family and founder of M.A. Kharafi & Sons, which would become one of Kuwait's largest business conglomerates. His sons would go on to become prominent figures in Kuwaiti business and politics. The company he established grew to dominate several sectors of the Kuwaiti economy, including construction, telecommunications, and investment. His legacy continued through his children, who became influential leaders in their respective fields, with the family business remaining a major force in Kuwait's economic landscape for generations.
achievements: Founded M.A. Kharafi & Sons

figures.name: Faiza Mohammed Al-Kharafi
figures.title: President of Kuwait University
figures.roleDescription: Chemist, academic, and first woman to head a major Middle Eastern university
figures.significance: First woman to head a major university in the Middle East and vice president of the World Academy of Sciences.
figures.biography: Faiza Mohammed Al-Kharafi was born to a wealthy family in Kuwait in 1946 and developed an interest in science from a young age. She received her BSc from Ain Shams University in Cairo in 1967, then attended Kuwait University where she founded the Corrosion and Electrochemistry Research Laboratory while in graduate school. She received her master's in 1972 and her PhD in 1975. She worked in Kuwait University's Department of Chemistry from 1975 to 1981, became chair of the department in 1984, and was Dean of the Faculty of Science from 1986 to 1989. On 5 July 1993, Emir Jaber Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah appointed her as rector of the university, making her the first woman to head a major university in the Middle East. She served as president from 1993 to 2002, overseeing 1,500 staff members and over 20,000 students. Al-Kharafi is a renowned electrochemist who studied corrosion and metal behavior, collaborated on discovering molybdenum-based catalysts, and became vice president of The World Academy of Sciences. She is married to Ali Mohammed Thanian Al-Ghanim and has five sons, including Marzouq Al-Ghanim, former speaker of Kuwait National Assembly.
achievements: First woman to head a major university in the Middle East
Vice president of the World Academy of Sciences
Founded Corrosion and Electrochemistry Research Laboratory

Named one of Forbes '100 Most Powerful Women'

figures.name: Mousa Abu Marzook
figures.title: Deputy Chairman of Hamas Political Bureau
figures.roleDescription: Senior Hamas political leader and international relations chief
figures.significance: Deputy Chairman of Hamas Political Bureau and key figure in Hamas's external leadership structure.
figures.biography:
Mousa Abu Marzook is a prominent Palestinian political leader who serves as Deputy Chairman of the Hamas Political Bureau and heads the group's international relations office as of 2023. He has been a central figure in Hamas's external leadership structure, operating from exile and directing operations from Damascus and later Egypt as part of what became known as the Gaza group of Hamas's external leadership.

Abu Marzook has been instrumental in Hamas's diplomatic and political activities, including participation in ceasefire negotiations during the Gaza war. In 2007, he explained Hamas's position regarding Israel, stating that while Hamas did not recognize Israel as a state, it considered Israel's existence as 'amr waqi' (fait accompli), describing this as 'de facto recognition' of Israel. He has been involved in prisoner exchange negotiations and has represented Hamas in various international forums. In early 2025, he was reportedly targeted in an Israeli airstrike on Doha along with other senior Hamas officials, though he survived the attack.
achievements: Deputy Chairman of Hamas Political Bureau
Head of Hamas international relations office
Key figure in Hamas external leadership structure

In 2015, he was awarded the Military Order of Achievement (Second Class), an honor established in 1995 by Sultan Qaboos bin Said to recognize officers who have served for 25 years. This award, which coincided with Sultan Qaboos's 25th anniversary on the throne, recognizes his significant contribution to Oman's defense forces and his exemplary military career.

figures.name: Ibrahim al-Rawas
figures.title: Baghdadi Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Religious leader who initiated restoration of Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque
figures.significance: Founded the al-Rawas family's custodianship of the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque and established their role in the Rifa'i Order in Baghdad.
figures.biography:
Ibrahim al-Rawas was a prominent Baghdadi sheikh from the Rifa'i Order who played a crucial role in the restoration of Islamic heritage in late 19th century Baghdad. Around 1887, he undertook a significant journey to Istanbul to meet with Ottoman Sheikh al-Islam Abu al-Huda al-Sayyadi to discuss restoring the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque, which had fallen into disrepair.

His diplomatic efforts proved successful, leading to a royal decree issued by Ottoman Sultan Abdul-Hamid II years later for the mosque's renovation at a cost of 4,000 Ottoman liras. In 1892, the mosque was completely renovated under the Sultan's orders, with Ibrahim al-Rawas establishing his family's hereditary custodianship of the complex. The renovated mosque included a new madrasa overlooking al-Rashid Street, and featured appointed religious officials including an Imam, preacher, muezzin, and caretaker.

Starting with Ibrahim al-Rawas, the noble Rifa'i Baghdadi family of al-Rawas assumed custodianship of the mosque and taught in the madrasa. His initiative established a lasting legacy, as the mosque has remained under the management of the al-Rawas family since its restoration, serving as the headquarters for the Sufi Rifa'i Order in Baghdad.
achievements: Successfully negotiated with Ottoman authorities for mosque restoration
Established al-Rawas family custodianship of Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque
Founded the second madrasa at the mosque complex

figures.name: Baha al-Din al-Rawas
figures.title: Rifa'i Ascetic
figures.roleDescription: Sufi mystic and ascetic of the Rifa'i Order
figures.significance: Represented the family's deep involvement in Sufi mysticism and ascetic practices within the Rifa'i Order.
figures.biography:
Baha al-Din al-Rawas was a devoted Rifa'i ascetic who dedicated his life to Sufi mysticism and spiritual practices. As a member of the prominent al-Rawas family, he embodied the family's deep connection to the Rifa'i Order and their commitment to Islamic mysticism and asceticism.

Originally, Baha al-Din was buried in his own tomb, likely connected to the original al-Rawas Mosque complex in the Bab al-Sheikh district of Baghdad. His burial site would have been a place of reverence for followers of the Rifa'i Order and members of the local community who respected his spiritual achievements and ascetic lifestyle.

When the al-Rawas Mosque was demolished in 1954, Baha al-Din's remains were carefully moved to the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque, where he was reburied next to Sayyid Sultan Ali himself. This relocation honored his spiritual status and maintained the continuity of the al-Rawas family's connection to the Rifa'i Order's headquarters in Baghdad, where his tomb can still be visited today.
achievements: Achieved recognition as a Rifa'i ascetic and mystic
Maintained family tradition of Sufi spiritual leadership

figures.name: Abdullah al-Rawas
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Contemporary religious leader and mosque supervisor
figures.significance: Supervised the modern reopening of Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque in 2016, continuing the family's custodial tradition.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Abdullah al-Rawas represents the contemporary generation of the al-Rawas family's religious leadership in Baghdad. As a nobleman from the Rifa'i Order, he has maintained the family's traditional role as custodians of the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque, continuing a responsibility that began with his ancestor Ibrahim al-Rawas in the late 19th century.

In 2016, Sheikh Abdullah al-Rawas supervised a significant reopening of the Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque, demonstrating his active leadership in preserving and maintaining this important religious site. His involvement in this reopening ceremony highlighted the continuity of the al-Rawas family's dedication to their custodial duties despite the challenges faced by Iraq's religious institutions in recent decades.

Sheikh Abdullah's role extends beyond mere administration, as he continues the family tradition of serving the Rifa'i Order and maintaining the mosque as the headquarters for the Sufi order in Baghdad. His leadership ensures that the mosque continues to serve as both a place of worship and a center for Rifa'i spiritual activities, preserving the religious and cultural heritage that his family has protected for over a century.
achievements: Supervised the reopening of Sayyid Sultan Ali Mosque in 2016
Maintained family custodianship tradition into the 21st century
